title: New Tesla Model Y v BYD Sealion: Ultimate review!
description:
Save up to £3750 on an EV with the Electric Car Grant: https://bit.ly/Electric-Car-Grant-2025

It’s time for a showdown between two of the best mid-sized electric SUVs you can buy in 2025 - the Tesla Model Y vs the BYD Sealion! 

The question is, which will come out on top in this all-new group test? Let’s take a closer look at the two contenders. 

The new Model Y has been updated both inside & out compared to the previous model. On the outside, there’s a Cybertruck-inspired front light bar, new alloy wheel designs, and a redesigned rear bumper, coupled with an illuminated Tesla badge. On the inside, it’s still much of the same as in the old car, apart from a few minor tweaks and changes. Thankfully, there’s also still a stalk for the indicator, unlike in the new Model 3! 

In terms of price, the Model Y range starts from £44,990, rising to £52,000 for the top-of-the-range edition. The car we have here is the long-range edition, which has dual motors for AWD, 507hp, 494Nm, and a weight of 1,995kg. 

As for the BYD Sealion, it has a pretty imposing look from the outside, with large air intakes on either side of the front bumper. Around the back, there’s a small boot lip spoiler, as well as a full-length light bar. On the inside, there’s a number of differences to the Tesla. There’s leatherette throughout the cabin that gives it a smart feel, while there are a number of physical buttons and a drive mode select lever. There’s a large infotainment screen that can rotated, and the BYD also comes with a set of digital dials. 

The BYD starts from £47,000, rising to around £59,000 for the range-topping edition. We had the dual motor Excellence edition for this test, which delivers 530hp and 690Nm, and weighs in at 2,420kg. 

The long-term trip computers on these cars recorded energy consumption figures of 3.7 miles per kWh in the Tesla, and 2.5 miles per kWh in the BYD. The Tesla's 75kWh battery means it should manage around 278 miles on a full charge, while the BYD's 91.3kWh battery should deliver around 228 miles. 

So will the BYD come out on top? Or will the Tesla reign supreme? You’ll need to stick with Mat to see for yourself!

The 2026 Tesla Model Y offers a practical balance of size, driving range, and price. With some exterior styling updates, most changes are similar to those made to the refreshed Model 3, which we tested last year. Still, the Model Y remains competitive among electric SUVs, thanks to quick charging, a smoother ride, and access to Tesla's extensive Supercharger network.

title: New Tesla Model Y: What have they done?
description:
Save up to £3750 on an EV with the Electric Car Grant: https://bit.ly/Electric-Car-Grant-2025

The Tesla Model Y has been updated! 

But this is no ordinary facelift - there are a whole load of changes both inside and out! Mat’s had a chance to get his hands on one before the official unveiling, and he’s about to run you through what’s changed. 

In terms of the design, there have been a whole load of updates. There’s a new bonnet and new wings on either side, and the headlights have been updated as well. A light bar has been added, too, which is giving serious Cybertruck vibes! Along the side, the only changes are new alloy designs, while around the back there’s an updated spoiler, new tail lights that reflect the light across the badge, and a new diffuser. All in all, the looks are a serious improvement over the original Y! 

On the inside the design is almost identical to the new Model 3. However, there’s one BIG change - the indicator stalk has returned in place of buttons on the steering wheel! Elsewhere, the changes are the same as in the Model 3. The seats are comfier and are now ventilated, and the dash design has been updated too. 

When it comes to performance, it comes with the same batteries and motors as the previous car. So this Launch Edition Model Y has dual motors to produce 384hp, as well as a 75kWh battery. There will be a single-motor edition, but the stats are yet to be revealed. 

The previous Model Y had a price tag of £52,000. In contrast, this new edition starts at £61,000 - a £9,000 increase! So is it worth it? You’ll need to stick with Mat to see for yourself!

title: Tesla Model Y L 2026 review Australia: Six-seat electric car changes family SUV game!
description:
CarsGuide journalist Andrew Chesterton reviews the 2026 Tesla Model Y L to see how this stretched six-seat electric SUV handles Australian roads. This new flagship variant sits above the standard Model Y and features a 150mm longer wheelbase to accommodate a 2+2+2 seating layout. We examine the single Premium Long Range AWD grade which offers a class-leading 681km WLTP range and 378kW of power.
The video covers the exterior design changes including the unique 19-inch Machina wheels and the addition of vehicle-to-load technology. Andrew tests the practicality of the second-row captain’s chairs and the power-folding third row to measure total cargo capacity. We also explore the updated cabin featuring an 18-speaker audio system and the 16-inch touchscreen that now houses the gear selector.
Our driving impressions focus on the new adaptive damping system and the 5.0-second sprint to 100km/h. Andrew compares the value proposition against rivals like the Kia EV9, Hyundai Ioniq 9 and Mercedes-Benz EQB to see if the $74,900 price point hits the mark. This review provides a factual look at the features, range and local ownership costs for Australian buyers.

Design, features & tech

Ground clearance, comfort & NVH

Minimalist cabin with a massive 15.4-inch central touchscreen

Heated & ventilated front seats, heated rear seats

title: Tesla Model 3 và tất cả những điều bạn cần biết - Xe điện giá rẻ đầu tiên
description:
Tesla Model 3 và tất cả những điều bạn cần biết - Kẻ đặt lại luật chơi xe điện

Từ năm 2017, Model 3 biến xe điện từ “đồ chơi xa xỉ” thành phương tiện phổ thông, buộc cả ngành lao vào cuộc đua điện hóa. Nhưng đằng sau ánh hào quang là vô số tranh cãi: Autopilot liên quan tai nạn chết người, triệu hồi hàng triệu xe, chất lượng hoàn thiện kém, và những quyết định gây sốc như bỏ cần số, bỏ xi-nhan.

Trong video này, chúng ta sẽ khám phá:
 • Vì sao Model 3 được gọi là “kẻ đặt lại luật chơi” của ngành ô tô.
 • Những bê bối và “production hell” khiến Elon Musk phải ngủ ngay sàn nhà máy.
 • Tác động của Model 3 đến thị trường: từ VinFast VF 8, Hyundai Ioniq 5, BYD Atto 3 cho đến Mercedes EQE, BMW iX.
 • Liệu Model 3 là anh hùng mở đường cho thời đại xe điện, hay cũng là kẻ phản diện với hàng loạt scandal?

title: Living with a Tesla Model 3 | Real World Review | Is It Worth Buying?
description:
This week on the DoneDeal YouTube channel we review the Tesla Model 3. This is the new version that came out in 2024. Despite the recent negative publicity, the Model 3 was the best selling EV in Ireland in June 2025 and so we wanted to see what all the fuss was about.

 It has lots of great upgrades from the previous generation,  however it arguably is missing some great features.

What's new?
- New Body Styling.
- New lights. 
- Acoustic rear glass. 
- Improved suspension. 
- Better tyres. 

On the flip side, they have removed the indicator stalk - and that is annoying! 

Overall, the Model 3 was a dream to live with. The charging network is super, the drive was excellent and we understand why people are still; purchasing them in 2025. 

Thanks for watching.

title: Tesla Model Y 2025 - Consumo y recarga real | Prueba | Test | Review en español | Diariomotor
description:
En esta prueba a fondo del Tesla Model Y 2025, también conocido como "Juniper" incorporamos una medida de consumo real a 120 km/h y una medición del tiempo de recarga a 120 km/h, lo que nos dará una idea precisa de su capacidad para viajar en el mundo real.

La capacidad para viajar suele ser el punto más débil de cualquier coche eléctrico y Tesla tiene fama de lograr los consumos más bajos y las autonomías más altas, así que en esta prueba del Tesla Model Y vamos a poner números reales sobre la mesa y vamos a calcular cuánto llevaría un viaje de 1.200 km con este coche.

Aparte de recorrer más de 70 km a velocidad constante para medir el consumo real en autopista a 120, en esta prueba verás todos los detalles del Tesla Model Y, por dentro, por fuera, plazas traseras, maletero y comportamiento al volante.

La versión probada es el tope de gama con tracción integral y 514 CV de potencia eléctrica a tope de equipamiento pero con las llantas de serie de 19".

title: 2025 Tesla Cybertruck Review: What’s It Like To Live With?
description:
The Tesla Cybertruck seems designed to polarize. Even though it’s been on sale for well over a year, the Cybertruck is as provocative as ever. Its beyond-radical, wedge-shaped body and stainless-steel body panels make it almost impossible to ignore, but what’s it like to live with in everyday use? Cars.com Senior Research Editor Damon Bell drove a 2025 Cybertruck AWD for a week to find out. Damon’s testing didn’t include off-roading, towing or hauling; he simply used the Cybertruck as a daily driver. How do the Cybertruck’s outlandish styling, unorthodox controls and ultra-quick steering affect its usability? What’s its interior room and cargo space like? Check out the full video to see.

title: We Drive the Base Model Y. Yes, $5000 Rebate! // 2026 Tesla Model Y RWD
description:
This is a Motormouth couple car review of the 2026 Tesla Model Y RWD base model that qualifies for the $5000 rebate in Canada.
In Canada, the 2026 Tesla Model Y RWD starts at $49,990 which means it qualifies for the Federal EV rebate of $5,000. In the US, there are two RWD models to choose from the base starts at $39,990
and Premium RWD $44,990.
 To keep the price point down and more affordable there are some differences between this base 2026 Tesla Model Y RWD model and the Premium trim. The exterior lightbar in the front and rear is not available, instead you get slim LED headlamps, black 18” wheels are standard and three exterior colours are offered, grey, black and white. For the interior, the glass roof is closed, a black interior is offered, manual recline rear seats (slight recline) and manual fold flat rear seats. 
The 2026 Tesla Model Y RWD does come with some nice standard features including, a 16” touchscreen, 7-speaker audio system, two wireless phone chargers, cloth and leatherette upholstery, heated front seats, heated steering wheel, closed glass roof, a hands-free power liftgate, 3 USB-C ports up to 65 watts and 9 exterior cameras. The base model does not have ventilated fronts seats, heated rear seats and ambient lighting. 
The base 2026 Tesla Model Y RWD model has 463 km/321 miles of range. It has a top speed of 201 km/h, 125 mph and goes 0-100 km/h in 7.2 seconds, 0-60 in 6.8 seconds.
In the US, there is a Premium 2026 Tesla Model Y RWD model with more range 357 miles and it’s faster 0-60 in 5.4 seconds. 
The base 2026 Tesla Model Y RWD trim comes with passive shock absorbers and the Premium AWD model frequency dependent shock absorbers. There is a slight difference in ride quality but the 18” tires with higher sidewalls offered on this model do a good job improving ride comfort and absorbing bumps. 
 At a Tesla Supercharger, you get up to 243 km/151 miles of range in just 15 minutes. 
There is a subscription for full-self driving for $99/month. A 30-day free trial is included.

title: 2025 Tesla Model Y Review: Better, Cheaper, and Still the EV to Beat
description:
The 2025 Tesla Model Y is here with a major refresh, and it’s not just cosmetic. With sharper styling, improved ride quality, longer range, new tech, and a lower starting price, Tesla’s best-selling SUV just got even more competitive. In this full review, we explore everything new about the Model Y, including the redesigned front and rear, more efficient aerodynamics, new suspension, better interior materials, and the addition of a rear seat display.

Tesla’s charging infrastructure remains a key advantage, with 250 kW DC fast charging and unrivaled Supercharger access across the country. Range estimates are strong across the board, with up to 350 miles available depending on the configuration. The upgraded seats, added sound insulation, improved climate control, and overall refinement make the 2025 Model Y feel more premium than ever — without a premium price increase.

Starting at $44,990 in the US, the Model Y continues to dominate its segment. Whether you’re cross-shopping the Hyundai Ioniq 5, Ford Mustang Mach-E, or Kia EV6, the new Tesla Model Y sets a high bar for electric family SUVs.

title: Tesla Model Y Long Range 2026 | In-depth Review | Still The Benchmark To Beat??
description:
In this in-depth Tesla Model Y 2026 review, we cut through the hype to find out exactly why the Tesla Model Y remains one of the best-selling electric SUVs in the world — and one of the most talked-about EVs in the UK.

The Model Y Long Range has been one of the most popular EVs for years now, but is it still the benchmark electric family SUV in 2026? Or are rivals finally catching up? Tom hasn’t always been a Tesla believer. The minimalist approach, the touchscreen-heavy controls, the hype around Autopilot — he’s been sceptical. But after spending real time living with the Model Y Long Range, could Tesla’s no-nonsense technology and real-world usability finally win him over?

In this full UK review, we cover everything you actually want to know about owning a 2026 Tesla Model Y:

-Interior quality, comfort & minimalist design
-Updated infotainment system, software & over-the-air updates
-Real-world performance and acceleration
-How Tesla’s semi-autonomous driving tech works on UK roads
-Boot space, rear seat practicality & family usability
-Running costs, efficiency & long-distance comfort
-Real-world range test (motorway + mixed driving)

title: Smart #5 review: should the Tesla Model Y be worried?
description:
Sell your car for free with Auto Express: https://bit.ly/AEX-Sell-My-Car

Say hello to the all-new Smart #5: a big, bold all-electric SUV from the brand known for making tiny city cars. Its chief rival is the globally best-selling Tesla Model Y, but it's also going up against the Skoda Enyaq, Hyundai Ioniq 5 and Audi Q4 e-tron, among others.

We think the #5 has more than a fighting chance against them. It's incredibly spacious, filled with technology, handles remarkably well and is capable of charging faster than pretty much any other EV on the road. Plus the Brabus version has nearly 640bhp and can do 0-62mph in 3.8 seconds!

The Smart #5 isn't going to arrive in the UK until close to the end of 2025, so our man Ellis hopped over to sunny Portugal to find out if the Model Y should start sleeping with one eye open...

title: New 2026 Tesla Model Y L Review | Tesla's Luxury 6-Seater SUV Is Here!
description:
Tesla Model Y L Review 2026 | Tesla's Luxury 6-Seater SUV Is Here!
Today, we’re Reviewing the Tesla Model Y L 2026, Tesla's Luxury 6-Seater SUV which everyone’s been talking about. The brand-new 2026 Tesla Model YL is built on the success of the Model Y, the Model YL isn’t just a stretched version, it’s a fully reimagined, family-focused SUV with premium upgrades across design, space, and performance. We’ll explore how the Model Y L takes everything people love about the Model Y and elevates it into a true luxury six-seater experience. From styling to tech, cargo to comfort, this is the ultimate Model Y evolution. Let’s dive in!

Exterior Evolution – Front Fascia & Design

- The Model YL showcases a refreshed front design, mirroring the premium aesthetics seen on the Juniper Model Y refresh, such as a sleeker, more streamlined nose and a minimalistic bumper. 
- The new star gold paint option adds a high-end flair that instantly elevates perceived exclusivity.
- A standout new feature: a single daytime running light bar spans the front, offering both style and distinctive identity.
- For practical visibility, Tesla has added a front bumper camera with an ultra-wide view and a self-cleaning nozzle—a smart solution for harsh weather.

Stretching the Silhouette – Side Profile

- The “L” in Y L stands for “long”: the wheelbase stretches 150 mm (≈6 in) longer, and overall length increases by 179 mm (≈7 in).
- This extension isn’t just about space—it gives the Y L a more grounded, elegant presence and completely alters its side profile.
- The 19-inch Mecha Wheels feature an aerodynamic star-pattern that helps preserve range despite added mass.

Rear Design & Aerodynamics 

- At the back, the Model Y L embraces the modern full-width taillight aesthetic seen across Tesla's latest models. 
- The revamped C-pillar and a more pronounced ducktail spoiler enhance high-speed stability using form-follows-function design.
- Updated Model YYY badging subtly nods to its three-row, six-seat layout with a clever twist.

Space & Cargo – The Family Advantage 

- The six-seat (“2+2+2”) layout shines. Even with all seats upright, there's space for multiple grocery bags—but fold the second and third rows, and cargo swells to a massive 2,539 liters (≈89.7 cu ft). 
- The YL maintains the front frunk for small essentials—a practical bonus for family trips.

Interior Luxury – Comfort for All Rows 

- Third Row: Heated seats, air vents on C-pillars, simplistic manual door handles, and fold buttons—cozy for kids or short adult trips, though headroom and thigh support remain constrained. 
- Second Row: Premium captain’s chairs with heating, ventilation, powered armrests, and an electric slide feature—offering first-class experience.
- A central 8-inch screen empowers second-row occupants to control climate, entertainment, and even the front passenger seat.
- Overhead, the seamless acoustic glass roof bathes the cabin in light while keeping noise minimal.

Front Row & Tech – Driver & Passenger Enhancements

- Front seats are sculpted for long-haul comfort—featuring adjustable headrests and thigh extensions, plus heating and ventilation.
- The minimalist dashboard is accented with aluminum trims, ambient lighting, and a premium black headliner.
- Tesla retains the classic indicator stalk, a win for intuitive control in roundabouts. 
- The center console features dual wireless pads (50 W driver-side, 30 W passenger-side) with active cooling—ideal for fast-charging smartphones.
- Dominating control is the 16-inch touchscreen—slightly larger than the standard Model Y's 15.4-inch display, sharper, and supremely responsive.

Performance & Range Metrics 

- Under the hood lurks a dual-motor AWD system, producing about 340 kW (~456 hp). 
- You’ll nail 0–100 km/h in 4.5 seconds and achieve 201 km/h (≈125 mph) top speed.
- The 82 kWh NMC battery delivers a 751 km CLTC range (~466 mi). 
Wikipedia
- Quick-charging capability keeps it practical for long drives—Tesla claims strong range recovery during fast charging sessions.

Pricing & Market Strategy 

- Launching in China, the Model Y L starts at 339,000 RMB (~$47,000 USD)—about 8% more than a standard Model Y. 
- Deliveries begin September 2025, with strong demand already reported.
- Elon Musk hinted the Y L might not reach U.S. markets, depending on autonomous-driving rollout. 
- The move counters local Chinese competitors like Li Auto and Xiaomi as Tesla reignites its presence in a shrinking market.

title: This BMW has a BIG PROBLEM
description:
Explore new and used cars with Carwow: https://bit.ly/Change-Your-Car-2804

It’s time for three of the most sought-after electric SUVs to go head-to-head to see which deserves a spot on your driveway. 

In one corner, we have the global best-seller - and recently updated - Tesla Model Y. It is being challenged by a fresh, high-tech rival from Ingolstadt, the Audi Q6 Sportback e-tron, and the latest all-electric offering from Munich, the BMW iX3. 

These three represent the cutting edge of premium family transport, but they each take a very different approach to design, performance, and daily usability.

The Tesla Model Y AWD Long Range remains the benchmark that everyone else is trying to beat. Known for its class-leading efficiency and unparalleled charging infrastructure, the Model Y offers a dual-motor setup that provides sure-footed all-wheel-drive traction and a 0-60mph time that puts many sports cars to shame. 

Inside, it’s as minimalist as every car in Tesla’s line-up, dominated by a single massive touchscreen that controls almost every function of the car. While some may find the lack of physical buttons polarising, there is no denying the sheer amount of interior space and the clever storage solutions, including a massive "frunk" and a cavernous rear boot.

Moving to the Audi Q6 Sportback RWD Performance, we see a focus on sleek aesthetics and long-distance touring. By opting for the rear-wheel-drive "Performance" variant, Audi has prioritised range, utilising a large battery and a more aerodynamic Sportback silhouette to extract maximum miles from every charge. 

Built on the new PPE platform shared with Porsche, the Q6 features an ultra-fast 800V charging architecture, allowing you to add hundreds of miles in the time it takes to grab a coffee. The interior features Audi’s new "Digital Stage" OLED display layout, although we’re not a fan of the huge bezels around the screen! Thankfully, though, there are high-quality materials throughout. 

The BMW iX3 50 xDrive represents the latest evolution of BMW's electric SUV lineup, now boasting a dual-motor configuration for significantly more punch than its predecessor. BMW has focused heavily on the driving experience, ensuring that even as a heavy SUV, it retains that signature "Ultimate Driving Machine" DNA with sharp steering and composed handling. 

It also features a massive battery capacity that pushes its range toward the 500-mile mark in ideal conditions, and like the Audi, it utilises an 800V system for lightning-fast top-ups. The cabin features the all-new panoramic iVision display, which, while impressive, potentially offers a lot of information that isn’t that useful on a trip to the shops! 

This group test isn't just about the numbers on a spec sheet; it’s about how these cars handle the real world. Mat will be putting all three through their paces, testing everything from their real-world efficiency and interior quality to their infotainment systems and rear-seat practicality. By the end of this deep dive, we’ll see if the Tesla can hold onto its crown or if the German giants have finally caught up to - and overtaken - the most popular electric SUV of recent years!

title: The Cheapest Model Y Is The Only Tesla You Need | Full Review
description:
Voices on the internet say the Tesla Model Y Standard is a variant to avoid. They argue that all the equipment it loses in the quest for improved affordability is enough to make it not worth considering. But is that really true?

Most of the criticism boils down to one idea: Tesla cut comfort and convenience features and that all adds up to make it feel cheap. So I treated the Standard like a normal car for almost two weeks, waiting for the moment it started to feel compromised and like a bad choice. It never came.

Sure, it’s not as nice as the Model Y Premium, whose suite of features does make it feel a cut above, but the Standard is still a lot of electric crossover for the money. Here in Romania, where I tested it, its €39,990 price tag before any incentives or discounts are applied makes it a very attractive buy. 

(Note: In the U.S., Tesla no longer calls this the “Standard,” instead labeling it the Model Y, with the more typical version now called the Model Y Premium. For clarity, we refer to it as the Standard throughout.)

You can’t get another similar EV here for less, and even in Standard trim, the Model Y has more standard equipment than more expensive rivals like the very popular Skoda Enyaq, which you see a lot around here (starting at just under €43,000).

What €39,990 buys you is the same body, the same packaging, and the same core usability as the regular Model Y, including access to the vast network of Superchargers, a genuinely massive cargo area and more performance than other base models.

The only other electric crossover that starts around the same price point in Europe is the Ford Explorer EV (based on Volkswagen’s MEB platform), but it’s a foot shorter, smaller inside, and not particularly premium-feeling. Probably the best alternative here in Europe is a base Hyundai Ioniq 5, which has a larger battery and more range. It only costs from €41,000, delivering 354 miles (570 km) WLTP on one charge, while the Tesla can only reach 331 miles (534 km), albeit with a smaller battery pack.

It’s a slightly different story in the U.S., where you can get rivals for less than its $39,990 starting price. The Chevrolet Equinox EV, Toyota bZ and Ford Mustang Mach-E in base trim all undercut the Model Y Standard and offer comparable specs. The Standard, therefore, makes more sense in Europe than in the States, where more affordable options are available.

But even if it’s not as cheap as rivals and in the U.S., does that mean you should avoid the Model Y Standard?

title: BYD Sealion 7 Review! - Better than a Tesla Model Y?
description:
🚗 BYD Sealion 7: My 4-Day Experience!

I’ve been driving the BYD Sealion 7 for the past few days, and here’s what stood out. First off, it’s a full EV with an 83 kWh battery, and I have to say—the design is solid. It’s sleek on the outside with flush door handles that add a premium touch, and inside, it’s spacious and well-thought-out. The rotating infotainment screen is a highlight, making everything from Apple CarPlay to car settings super accessible. However, the driver’s screen? A bit overloaded with information—BYD might want to refine that. The 50W wireless charging pad (with built-in cooling!) is a great touch, and storage is surprisingly generous, with deep pockets that can be adjusted as needed. Comfort-wise, the Nappa leather seats (on the higher trim) are excellent, offering great support for both front and rear passengers. The back seats? Equally spacious and comfortable, making it a solid family-friendly choice.

Now, let’s talk about performance. The Performance Edition hits 0-100 km/h in 4.5 seconds, while the Premium Edition does it in 6.7 seconds. But realistically, do you need that extra punch? Probably not. What really matters is the range—around 450 km—which has been quite accurate in my experience. I drove 200 km, and I still had about 50% charge left. The car also comes with app support, letting you control key features remotely. And then there’s the price: AED 192,000 for the Performance Edition vs. AED 172,000 for the Premium Edition. Honestly, the Premium Edition seems like the smarter buy—you gain extra range, lose a bit of speed, and trade Nappa leather for regular seats, but everything else stays the same.

So, is the BYD Sealion 7 worth it? If you’re looking for a well-priced, fully electric SUV with solid range, a premium feel, and modern tech, it’s definitely worth considering. But hey, that’s just my take—what do you think? 👀💬

title: Test av Tesla Model 3 Bakhjulsdrift: Gjerrig og morsom billigbil
description:
Bilen vi tester her er den enkleste og billigste i hele Tesla-utvalget.

Sist vi testet tilsvarende modell var for to år siden, og modellen er faktisk blitt enda litt billigere. 

Denne får du under 350.000 kroner, uten at vi dveler spesielt ved rentekampanjer og denslags.

I og med at vi har sett nøye på Model 3 ved en rekke anledninger, dveler vi heller ikke ved alle detaljer her (noen av dem tar vi dog med i artikkel på elbil.no).

Viktigste endring siden faceliften høsten 2023, under kjøring, er at blinklyshendelen er tilbake på venstre side av rattet.

Her ser vi på energiforbruk, kjøreegenskaper, hurtiglading og grunnleggende data om det Tesla ikke presenterer rett fram - altså utnyttbar batterikapasitet.

Det er uansett bare å fastslå at energiforbruket er blant de aller laveste vi noensinne har målt. 

Les utdypende artikkel på elbil.no: https://elbil.no/kjapp-test-av-teslas-billigste-model-3-utgave/

Is the facelifted Tesla Model Y Juniper a true game-changer or just a mild refresh spoiled by Elon Musk’s latest controversies? With a sharper design, more power and significantly improved suspension, Tesla’s best-seller aims for a three-peat as the world’s top-selling car after snagging that title in 2023 and 2024. 

But will Musk’s polarizing moves and a stock shortage hold it back?

We put the new Model Y through its paces, specifically the dual-motor Long Range AWD version, which now has up to 551km of range and 331kW on tap. It’s faster and more efficient than ever. But is it enough to fend off rising EV challengers like the BYD Sealion 7, Xpeng G6 and Zeekr 7X?

title: Saving Tesla dash cam footage without honking the horn!
description:
The Tesla dash cam feature is saving all the footage from the cameras around the car to the USB stick in the glovebox. But this is being constantly overwritten unless you specifically tell the car to keep the footage. The default method is to honk the horn and then the last 10 minutes of footage is saved and not overwritten. However, there are some instances where honking the horn would be confrontational or less than ideal. What you can do is drop the dashcam icon down to the shortcut bar, then while driving, a quick tap on that icon does the same and is far better than honking the horn.
Or you can use the voice control and say "save dash cam" or you can setup the left scroll wheel hold to be a shortcut to save the dash cam.
To learn more about Tesla's dash cam and Sentry mode, see the full video https://youtu.be/nwW2TEpiTTs
The vehicle is a 2024 Tesla Model Y RWD (HW3) with the 62.5kWh (60,5 kWh usable) LFP battery and when filming this was on software version 2025.26.4.
This video was filmed in August 2025.

title: Tesla Model Y new model review | Best selling car, bettered?
description:
#Tesla #TeslaModelY #ElonMusk 

In this in-depth review of the new Tesla Model Y launch edition, I dive into the significant upgrades that make this car a standout in the electric vehicle market. From improved handling and ride quality to a quieter cabin and better suspension, Tesla has worked hard to make the Model Y even more refined while maintaining its sporty edge.

Key upgrades include:

Enhanced suspension with better bushings, springs, and tires for a smoother ride
Improved sound insulation, including new Michelin tires and sound-deadening glass
Subtle design tweaks like a sleek, badge-less front end and an integrated ducktail spoiler
Practical interior upgrades, including USB-C charging ports and intuitive tech features
Increased driving comfort with a more refined brake-by-wire system and updated steering dynamics.

I also touch on the new Model Y's range, which includes impressive WLTP ratings, fast DC charging, and new cabin technology for a more premium driving experience. The changes are subtle but substantial, making the Model Y a more comfortable and enjoyable car for both drivers and passengers.

Is the new Tesla Model Y better than its predecessor? Find out in my detailed review and decide if this electric SUV is worth upgrading to.

title: Tesla Model Y STANDARD 2026 Night Lights Review
description:
Tesla Model Y STANDARD 2026 Night Lights Review

Model Y Standard focuses on what matters most to drivers — long range, versatility and features that deliver everyday convenience and entertainment. Model Y Standard retains Tesla’s DNA, with unrivaled software,
 leading efficiency, and top-tier safety – all while being the most affordable Model Y ever. 
 
As with all Tesla vehicles, the latest addition to the Model Y lineup comes standard with an extensive suite of features, including remote mobile app access, Trip Planner with real-time Supercharger availability,
 and a variety of gaming and entertainment options. Phone Key, Remote Climate Control, Sentry Mode, Dog Mode, and all the Tesla signature features are included. 
 
Model Y Standard delivers the same superior software experience as every other Model Y. Over-the-air updates continually enhance the vehicle, introducing new features and improving existing ones at no extra cost.
 It also comes standard with Autopilot and is hardware-ready for Full Self-Driving (Supervised), which will be enabled remotely on applicable vehicles once approved by local regulators. 

A distinctive new design, including specific front and rear fascias, gives Model Y Standard a unique identity within the lineup. In true Tesla fashion, these design choices were made to enhance efficiency and the
 overall driving experience.
 
The cabin offers a comfortable cocoon for all five passengers. Maximizing utility, Model Y Standard features new soft-touch, durable textile seats designed to perform in all scenarios and
 weather conditions. Inspired by early Model S and Cybertruck, a large storage space between the front seats provides room for everyday belongings, keeping them within easy reach for maximum usability and convenience. 

And when needed, Model Y Standard offers up to 835 liters of cargo space with five passengers, and 2,118 liters with the second row folded—enough to carry bikes, camping gear, or other large
 items. 

Maximizing Value for Money 
Another important aspect of the Model Y Standard’s design was guided by a focus on total cost of ownership. 

Tesla vehicles are known for their leading energy efficiency, and Model Y Standard is no exception—it is the most efficient Model Y ever. With a consumption of 13.1 kWh/100 km and a range of 534 km WLTP, owners can drive more kilometers per euro spent on charging.

title: Tesla Model Y L: It's not just the extra seats that make this the best Tesla
description:
Tesla Model Y L Review: 6 Seats, 681km Range, and an Easter Road Trip Without the Fuel Bill
Tesla's new six-seat, long-wheelbase Model Y L has arrived in New Zealand priced from $83,900, and we drove it from Mangawhai to Wellington over Easter to find out whether the biggest Y is also the best.
With petrol at $3.50 a litre and the Middle East fuel crisis making everyone nervous, the timing for a 681km-range EV could not be better. We covered 750km on the Easter long weekend with just 11 minutes of charging, averaging 15kWh/100km over the full trip and 10kWh/100km on the flatter sections.
In this review:
Pricing and what you get for $6,000 over the standard Model Y Long Range AWD
The 2-2-2 six-seat layout, captain's chairs, and a third row that actually works
New continuously variable adaptive damping and how it transforms the ride
Real-world efficiency and charging on a 750km road trip
Vehicle-to-load (V2L) capability — a Tesla NZ first
Grok AI, Autopilot, phantom braking, and what's missing without FSD
The verdict: is this the best Model Y you can buy?

2026 Tesla Model Y L Premium AWD spec:

Seats: 6 (2-2-2)

34:04 - Software Info: Ryzen, Heat Pump & LFP
36:00 - Final Thoughts: Is the Standard Y Worth It?

Model Y Standard focuses on what matters most to drivers — long range, versatility and features that deliver everyday convenience and entertainment. Model Y Standard retains Tesla’s DNA, with unrivaled software, leading efficiency, and top-tier safety – all while being the most affordable Model Y ever. 
 
As with all Tesla vehicles, the latest addition to the Model Y lineup comes standard with an extensive suite of features, including remote mobile app access, Trip Planner with real-time Supercharger availability, and a variety of gaming and entertainment options. Phone Key, Remote Climate Control, Sentry Mode, Dog Mode, and all the Tesla signature features are included. 
 
Model Y Standard delivers the same superior software experience as every other Model Y. Over-the-air updates continually enhance the vehicle, introducing new features and improving existing ones at no extra cost. It also comes standard with Autopilot and is hardware-ready for Full Self-Driving (Supervised), which will be enabled remotely on applicable vehicles once approved by local regulators. 

A distinctive new design, including specific front and rear fascias, gives Model Y Standard a unique identity within the lineup. In true Tesla fashion, these design choices were made to enhance efficiency and the overall driving experience.
 
The cabin offers a comfortable cocoon for all five passengers. Maximizing utility, Model Y Standard features new soft-touch, durable textile seats designed to perform in all scenarios and
 weather conditions. Inspired by early Model S and Cybertruck, a large storage space between the front seats provides room for everyday belongings, keeping them within easy reach for maximum usability and convenience. 

And when needed, Model Y Standard offers up to 835 liters of cargo space with five passengers, and 2,118 liters with the second row folded—enough to carry bikes, camping gear, or other large items. 

Maximizing Value for Money 
 
Another important aspect of the Model Y Standard’s design was guided by a focus on total cost of ownership. 

Tesla vehicles are known for their leading energy efficiency, and Model Y Standard is no exception—it is the most efficient Model Y ever. With a consumption of 13.1 kWh/100 km and a range of 534 km WLTP, owners can drive more kilometers per euro spent on charging.

title: Tesla Model Y Juniper 2026 REVIEW 🚗 Premium Long Range
description:
Ponemos a prueba el nuevo *Tesla Model Y Long Range,* la versión con tracción trasera que promete ser la compra más inteligente del año. Analizamos a fondo esta esperada actualización que destaca por su enorme *batería de 75 kilovatios hora,* logrando una eficiencia sobresaliente en viajes largos. Durante nuestro test comprobamos su comportamiento dinámico y ponemos a prueba la velocidad de carga en los supercargadores. Aunque mejora notablemente su suspensión y aplomo en autopista, valoramos objetivamente si la ausencia de un cuadro de instrumentos tradicional lastra la experiencia frente a competidores directos como el *Skoda Enyaq RS o BYD Seal U.* Descubre si el rey de los SUV eléctricos sigue dominando el mercado europeo.


*📊 RESUMEN TESLA MODEL Y LONG RANGE*

✅ *Lo mejor* - Espacio interior enorme y maleteros gigantes
_"Me flipa su enorme maletero donde puedes dejar todo lo que se te ocurra y las plazas traseras son espectacularmente amplias."_

❌ *Lo peor* - Ausencia de cuadro tradicional y diseño soso
_"El interior es muy minimalista y bien construido pero creo que es demasiado soso y echarás en falta un Head-Up Display."_

💡 *Veredicto* - El SUV eléctrico más racional para familias

title: Tesla Model Y Performance DIVERTE COME POCHE: Prova su strada
description:
La Tesla Model Y Performance costa oltre 60.000€. Una cifra importante. Ma quando premi l’acceleratore e senti 0-100 in 3,7 secondi, capisci subito che non stai guidando una normale SUV elettrica.
In questo video ti portiamo nella nostra prova su strada completa: autostrada, misto, città e qualche tratto sportivo per capire davvero cosa sa fare questo mezzo. Partiamo dal design aggiornato del restyling 2024, analizziamo interni e infotainment senza sconti sulla qualità dei materiali, poi entriamo nel vivo con la dinamica di guida nelle tre modalità disponibili — Comfort, Sport e Track.
Ma la vera domanda è un’altra: quanta autonomia fa realmente nel ciclo misto italiano? Niente cifre WLTP, solo quello che abbiamo misurato noi. Passiamo poi dalla ricarica al Supercharger con tempi e costi alla mano, fino al confronto diretto con la versione Long Range per capire se il sovrapprezzo della Performance ha senso o no.
Il verdetto finale potrebbe sorprenderti.
Se hai già guidato una Model Y Performance, scrivilo nei commenti: vogliamo sapere la tua esperienza reale. E se non vuoi perdere le prossime prove su strada, iscriviti al canale.

The 2026 Tesla Model Y "L" is finally real, and it fixes the biggest flaw of the world's best-selling EV. Coming soon to Europe and the US, this isn't just a facelift—it’s a 5-inch longer, 6-seater beast with a massive 466-mile range.

In this video, we review the new Model Y L (Long Wheelbase) to see if the new Captain's Chairs, "Star Gold" paint, six seats and upgraded suspension make this the ultimate family EV. Is it worth waiting for this over the standard Model Y Juniper update? Let's find out!

Range: 466 Miles (EPA Est.)
Battery: 82 kWh NMC

New Tech: Front bumper camera with washer, 50W cooled wireless charging, 8-inch rear screen.

Price: The Tesla Model Y L launched in China around $47,000 USD, with estimates suggesting it could land in the US around $51,000, Europe closer to €67,000, though exact pricing isn't set.

title: Mercedes FINALLY beats the Tesla Model 3? Mercedes CLA EQ 250+ first drive review
description:
It's taken a while, but Mercedes might have finally knocked the Tesla Model 3 off its perch.

The new electric CLA – or Mercedes CLA 250+ with EQ Technology to be exact – has a staggering 484 mile range with a fully charged battery.

It's also built with 800 volt architecture, much like the Porsche Taycan and Hyundai Ioniq 5, meaning it can rapid charge at some super quick 320kW speeds.

Mercedes has also filled it to the brim with technology, including an AI-enhanced new operating system that lets you talk to the car like it's a human being.

But is all this enough to beat the likes of the Tesla Model 3, BYD Seal, Polestar 2 and so on? We've been in Copenhagen to find out.

title: Viaje 617 km y 1 parada 7 min con Tesla Model 3 RWD 2025 nueva 🔋LFP 64 kWh #TeslaModel3
description:
Viajamos de Madrid (Torrejón de Ardoz) hasta Marbella (San Pedro de Alcántara) con el Tesla Model 3 Gran Autonomía Tracción Trasera (RWD) con batería LFP de 64 kWh brutos (60,5 kWh útiles).

Un coche que ofrece:
✅ Autonomía estimada en ciclo mixto: 554 km
✅ Potencia: 283 cv y 420 Nm
✅ Prestaciones: 0-100 km/h en 6,1 s
✅ Velocidad máxima 201 km/h
✅ Carga en AC: hasta 11 kW
✅ Carga en DC: hasta 250 kW (del 10% al 80% en 24 min)
✅ Precio: desde 35.000 € antes de ayudas (28.000 € con ayudas)


📍 En este viaje real de 617 km hemos conseguido:
🔋 452,6 km sin recargar (llegando con un 4% de batería)
⚡ Una única parada de 15 min en Moraleda de Zafayona (Granada)
🔋 Llegada a Marbella con un 32% de batería
⛽ Consumo medio: 11,8 kWh/100 km
💶 Coste total real: 0,57 €/100 km (2,12 €/100 km si pagamos el Supercharger)

👉 Con el programa de referidos de Tesla todavía es posible acceder a recargas gratis.

Viajar en coche eléctrico ya no es un reto: es barato, cómodo y rápido.

title: Xiaomi SU7 Ultra v Tesla Model S Plaid: DRAG RACE
description:
Our merch store is now LIVE: https://bit.ly/Carwow-Merchandise

It’s time for a China vs USA showdown! 

We’ve got our hands on a Xiaomi SU7 Ultra, and it’s going up against a Tesla Model S Plaid! It’s the first time we’ve featured a Xiaomi on this channel, but does it have what it takes to bring down the mighty Tesla? Let’s check out the stats…

First up, we have the Xiaomi. It’s powered by three electric motors, and these combine to produce an incredible 1,548hp and 1,770Nm of torque. It comes with all-wheel drive, and it weighs in at a pretty heavy 2,360kg. In terms of price, it costs ¥529,900, which is the equivalent of just £55,000!

Next up, it’s the Tesla. It’s equipped with three electric motors - one on the front axle, and two on the rear. Combined, they produce an almighty 1,020hp and 1,420Nm of torque. Naturally, the motors and batteries weigh it down a bit, so it tips the scales at 2,190kg. In terms of price, you can pick one up new for £113,000.

So the Xiaomi is up on power, but it’s also up on weight! So does it have what it takes to topple the mighty Tesla? There’s only one way to find out for sure… LET’S RACE!

title: 2026 Tesla Model Y L 6-Seater Review: Walkaround Pricing Specs & Drive
description:
Join Tom and Joy from Ludicrous Feed for an in-depth look at the Tesla Model Y L, the new six-seater variant now available in Australia. This comprehensive review covers every detail of the extended Model Y, which features a 150mm longer wheelbase and 177mm additional overall length compared to the five-seat version. The team explores the practical additions, including the 3.3 kW V2L capability—a first for Tesla in Australia—allowing the 82 or 88 kWh NMC battery to power your home or export to the grid. With a 681 km WLTP range, 250 kW DC charging, and a 0-100 km/h time of 5.0 seconds, this all-wheel-drive dual motor SUV maintains impressive performance. Tom and Joy demonstrate the electronically folding second and third-row seats, the flat-floor cargo configuration with underfloor storage, and test real-world luggage capacity. Inside, they examine the new powered and ventilated captain's chairs in the second row, manual vents for the middle and rear rows, adjustable headrests, and the updated 50W wireless charging pad with cooling. The review also covers the new ride and handling settings—balanced and rear comfort—tested from both the driver's seat and the third row, along with NVH levels on the highway. From the Cosmic Silver paint and Machina 2.0 wheels to the practicality of six seats in a footprint similar to the standard Model Y, this is a complete walkthrough of Tesla's latest family-focused EV | Video by Tom Gan (Tesla Tom) and Joy of Ludicrous Feed from Sydney Australia March 2026

title: How to Turn on Hazard Lights on Tesla Model Y & 3 - Easy Method
description:
You can turn on the hazard lights in a Tesla Model Y quickly by pressing the dedicated button on the touchscreen.

Step 1 — Locate the Hazard Light Button
The hazard light button in the Tesla Model Y is located on the center touchscreen at the top of the display, typically marked with a red triangle icon. There is no traditional physical switch on the steering column like in some vehicles.

Step 2 — Activate the Hazard Lights
Simply tap the red triangle icon on the touchscreen. When pressed, all four turn signal lights will start flashing simultaneously to alert other drivers.

Step 3 — Confirm Activation
Check that the rear and front turn signals are blinking; this indicates the hazard lights are active. The touchscreen icon may also highlight to reflect that the hazard signal is on. To turn off the hazard lights, tap the button again.

Following these steps ensures that your Model Y’s hazard lights are activated quickly and safely during emergencies or when stopped roadside.

description:
🎥 2026 Tesla Model 3 Long Range RWD (~283 HP) | 0-100 & Review | Joe Black

Meet the ultimate electric distance runner! 🔋🛣️
Today we are testing the **2026 Tesla Model 3** in the highly requested **Long Range Rear-Wheel Drive (RWD)** configuration. 
By combining the large battery pack from the AWD model with a single, highly efficient electric motor on the rear axle, Tesla has created an absolute range monster. This specific trim boasts a massive **750 km (WLTP)** range, making it one of the most efficient EVs on the global market today. 
It might not have the dual-motor punch, but a single motor pushing approximately **283 HP (208 kW)** to the rear wheels in a sleek, aerodynamic sedan is still a recipe for fun. How does it perform on the road?

🔥 JOE'S TEST REALITY CHECK:
Tesla claims a 0-100 km/h sprint in 5.2 seconds.
**My Dragy measurement (0-100 km/h): 5.3 seconds!** 📉
(Missing the factory claim by just 0.1 seconds is basically a perfect score, often dependent on the exact state of charge or tire temperature. For a single-motor car focused purely on maximum range, 5.3 seconds is still sports-sedan fast!).

⚡ **ENERGY CONSUMPTION & RANGE:**
The Long Range RWD is built for efficiency. With aerodynamic improvements and a lighter single-motor setup, it sips electricity. During my dynamic test, it still maintained incredible efficiency, proving why this version is the ultimate road-trip machine.

title: Tesla Model 3 STANDARD 2026 Night Test Drive
description:
Tesla Model 3 STANDARD 2026 Night Test Drive.

✅ Tesla Referral Code : http://ts.la/stupinean5809

Tesla Model 3 Standard 2026 – What’s New

The Tesla Model 3 Standard 2026 introduces a new entry-level version of Tesla’s popular electric sedan, designed to offer a more affordable price while keeping the core technology and driving experience of the Model 3. This variant focuses on efficiency and value while still delivering strong performance and access to Tesla’s software ecosystem and Supercharger network.  ￼

Rear-Wheel Drive Electric Powertrain

The Model 3 Standard uses a single electric motor mounted on the rear axle, producing around 208 kW (283 hp) and 420 Nm of torque. This configuration provides smooth acceleration and balanced driving dynamics typical of rear-wheel-drive electric vehicles.  ￼

Acceleration from 0–100 km/h takes around 6.1 seconds, while the top speed is approximately 201 km/h, making the Standard version quick enough for everyday driving.  ￼

Battery and Range

The Standard version uses a smaller battery pack of around 69.5 kWh, optimized for efficiency and lower cost. Despite the smaller battery, Tesla estimates a range of up to 321 miles (about 516 km) according to EPA testing, making it one of the most efficient electric sedans in its class.  ￼

Real-world range can vary depending on conditions, but estimates typically range between 365 km and more than 520 km depending on temperature and driving style.  ￼

Charging Capabilities

The Model 3 Standard supports Tesla’s Supercharger fast-charging network, with peak charging speeds of about 225 kW. This allows the battery to recharge quickly during long trips, making the car practical for daily use and long-distance travel.  ￼

Like other Tesla models, the vehicle also benefits from battery pre-conditioning, which automatically prepares the battery for optimal fast charging when navigating to a Supercharger.  ￼

Interior and Equipment Changes

To reduce costs, the Standard version simplifies some interior features compared with higher trims. Tesla replaces some premium materials with textile seat inserts, removes the rear passenger touchscreen, and reduces certain luxury features.  ￼

However, the car still retains key Tesla technology such as:
 • The 15.4-inch central touchscreen infotainment system
 • Tesla’s minimalist dashboard layout
 • Over-the-air software updates
 • Advanced driver-assistance features including Autopilot.  ￼

Exterior and Design

From the outside, the Model 3 Standard looks almost identical to the other Model 3 variants, featuring Tesla’s sleek aerodynamic design and the updated styling introduced with the Highland facelift.

The car typically comes with 18-inch wheels as standard, with optional 19-inch wheels available.  ￼

Position in the Model 3 Lineup

The Standard model serves as the entry point in the Model 3 lineup, sitting below the Premium and Long Range versions. By simplifying some features and slightly reducing battery capacity, Tesla is able to offer a lower price while maintaining the essential Tesla driving experience.  ￼

Conclusion

The Tesla Model 3 Standard 2026 focuses on efficiency, value and everyday usability. While it sacrifices some luxury features compared with higher trims, it still offers impressive range, strong performance and access to Tesla’s advanced technology ecosystem.

For many buyers, the Standard version represents one of the most accessible ways to enter the Tesla electric vehicle lineup while retaining the core strengths that made the Model 3 one of the world’s best-selling EVs.

Zeekr 7GT Review

Zeekr, Geely Group's most luxurious brand, has launched the 7GT, a station wagon version of its 007 sedan, in the European market. Using an 800-volt electrical architecture, the Zeekr 7GT supports charging power up to 480 kW, making it the electric car with the highest charging capacity sold in Europe.

What does the Zeekr 7GT offer?

The smaller 75 kWh battery can reach 80% charge from 10% in just 13 minutes. The longer-range 100 kWh battery reaches this level in 16 minutes. Zeekr states that with both battery options, a 10-minute charge provides a range of 340 km.

In its most powerful dual-motor version, the 7GT can accelerate from 0–100 km/h in 3.3 seconds. In the single-motor version, this time is 5.3 seconds. The maximum speed is limited to 210 km/h in all variants.

Although of Chinese origin, the 7GT clearly exhibits a European character in its design. This is because the model was designed in Sweden and specifically targeted at European users. With its simple and elegant design, far from exaggerated lines, it resembles European cars. In terms of charging technology, it has a more advanced infrastructure than all its European-made competitors.

However, it is not at the top of its class in terms of efficiency. The longest-range, rear-wheel-drive single-motor version offers a range of 655 km according to WLTP measurements. The entry-level model with a 75 kWh battery provides a range of 519 km, while the all-wheel-drive version with a larger battery can travel 558 km on a single charge.

Looking at the prices, the Business Edition RWD version starts at €45,990. The Long Range Launch Edition RWD is €50,990, and the top-of-the-line Privilege AWD has a price tag of €57,490. The car, which is already available for order, will begin deliveries in July.

title: TEST OF TESLA FSD in Rotterdam - April 2026
description:
What does Tesla’s Full Self-Driving look like in real conditions in Europe?
Beyond the demos and announcements, how does the system actually behave on the road?

In Rotterdam, we tested Tesla’s Full Self-Driving (FSD) in a real urban environment.
One of the first contexts in Europe where this technology is being explored on public roads (with driver supervision).

Through this test, this video captures how the system interacts with a complex European city:
cyclists, pedestrians, intersections, and everyday traffic situations.

Because behind the name “Full Self-Driving”, the reality is more nuanced.
This is not full autonomy yet, but an advanced driver assistance system that still requires constant human supervision.

This is not a lab test.
It is a real-world observation, based on field experimentation.

_______________________________________________________________________________________________

🎥 In this video, we explore:

- How Tesla’s FSD behaves in a European urban environment

How can you separate your battery-electric crossover from so many others? Fill it full of interesting and novel technology is a common response – and one that Lexus has indulged in with its updated RZ.

The brand’s plush EV that’s been hacked about with a blade has been given a new lease of life, with Lexus (and Toyota) engineers improving the car’s efficiency and using it as a springboard for some new driving technologies. So… does it all work?

At a glance
Pros: Balanced ride; steer-by-wire feels intuitive; impressive material quality; good space

Cons: Interior annoyances; steer-by-wire ‘yoke’ is badly laid out; ‘manual’ shift is flawed

What’s new?
On the face of it, it doesn’t look like the RZ has changed much at all. The design is very similar to what it was before, with super sharp headlights and lines in the front end, big wheels and a coupe-ish silhouette. Ditto the general look of the interior, with a high centre console, big glossy central screen and lots of great-feeling materials.

In fact, a lot of the RZ’s improvements aren’t all that visible. The battery pack and electric motors have seen improvements in efficiency – the same tweaks that are applied to the Toyota bZ4X – with new battery cooling and a slightly denser pack, as well as ‘eAxle’ motors that have been designed to reduce energy losses as much as possible. Lexus has also included some new soundproofing.

The versions available have changed – more on that in a bit – with the newest and most interesting bit being the introduction of an F Sport trim (pictured above): a performance-ish model designed to be a technological pioneer as much as a sporty variant.

We say that because this is the brand’s first production car available with steer-by-wire – a technology that Lexus has been developing for some years. The RZ F Sport model also features ‘Interactive Manual Drive’, which is code for virtual ‘gear’ shifting designed to make the driving experience more engaging. Think Hyundai Ioniq 5 N’s N-Shift and you’re pretty much there.

What are the specs?
There are now three power outputs available: 350e, 500e and 550e.

The entry-level 350e is likely to be the best-seller, generating 221bhp from a single electric motor and sprinting to 62mph in 7.5sec. This version claims the highest range, too; 353 miles for those on 18-inch wheels or 316 miles for models on 20-inch wheels.

The 500e comes with two e-motors with all-wheel drive, with performance jumping to 376bhp, the 0-62mph sprint dropping to 4.6 seconds and the range claims clocking in at 311 or 283 miles respectively.

Both the 350e and 500e can be had in Premium and Premium Plus specification, and you can choose which wheel size option you prefer. The RZ in general is very well equipped, with basic ones coming with loads of safety tech, dual-zone climate control, 360-degree parking aids, a panoramic glass roof, a wireless charger and more. Plus throws in a head-up display, memory seats and touch-sensitive steering wheel controls. Takumi trim is also available here, too, adding a Mark Levinson audio system among other luxuries.

Topping the powertrain options is the 550e model. It’s all-wheel drive, too, developing 402bhp and hitting 62mph in 4.4 seconds. This version is only available as an F Sport with 20-inch wheels, with Lexus claiming a 280-mile e-range.

You can only spec the 550e in F Sport trim. That means bespoke exterior detailing like aerodynamic wheels and a more aggressive bodykit, as well as sporty details and thickly-bolstered sports seats. All 550e F Sport models include steer-by-wire and the ‘interactive manual drive’ system, and there is an F Sport Takumi model that includes more luxuries.

What’s it like to drive?
We had the chance to sample all three power variants and try out all the new technology; our test cars were all on 20-inch wheels, too.

The 350e will be all anyone really needs here; not only does it offer the best range, but its performance is perfectly brisk enough –

title: Most Tesla Owners Choose the Wrong Color
description:
Thinking about a Tesla Model Y Performance? After owning mine for almost a month, I genuinely think Quicksilver is the most underrated—and possibly the best—color Tesla offers right now.

In direct sunlight, it shifts between silver, gray, and a liquid metallic look that stands out without being over the top. It also hides dirt better than darker colors and gives the Model Y a more premium feel.

I own a Tesla and help clients find the right luxury and performance vehicles through Myrtle Beach Automotive, including BMW and Mercedes-Benz. If you’re comparing options or want help finding the right vehicle, I’m happy to help.

Kia EV6 GT (2025) review

The facelifted Kia EV6 GT is more than just a useful bump in power output and a rearranged visage. This is only the second electric car to get a digitally defined virtual gearbox, following on from its close relation, the Hyundai Ioniq 5 N. The implementation is surprisingly different, however, and not necessarily as invigorating as you might expect. But then, the Kia is consciously a grand tourer, not a giant hot hatch.

On top of this, the new EV6 GT has a bigger battery, faster charging, and updated infotainment. Most remarkably of all, Kia is selling it for less – the £59,985 starting price representing a saving of £2,690 versus the pre-facelift model.

After two days running it ragged across some of Scotland’s most spectacular scenery – the Old Military Road being a particular highlight – we’ve already gotten to know the 2025 EV6 GT pretty well. Here’s how we found it.

At a glance
Pros: Faster and more comfortable than before, quicker charging, bigger battery, better inside, cheaper!
Cons: Virtual Gear Shift feels less essential that expected – but we’re still glad Kia’s added it

What’s new?
The Virtual Gear Shift (VGS) is the most obvious technical differentiator between this and the previous version, but it’s probably not where you’d notice the biggest difference every day. For the new model has an extra 64bhp to play with, bringing the total up to 641bhp.

Better yet, there’s a new ‘connect car Navigation Cockpit’ (ccNC) infotainment system that offers split-screen functionality and customisable shortcuts. Still a touch faffy in places but easy enough to get to grips with.

The new nose gains the same ‘star map’ lighting signature as the rest of the updated EV6 range, plus there are reworked bespoke bumpers and a new design of 21-inch alloy wheel. The neon green detailing inside and out – including for the brake calipers – we could do without but it’s distinctive.

Other changes in the cabin include revised ergonomics and the extermination of the previous piano-black trim, while lower spring rates and adjustments to the electronically controlled variable dampers aim to improve comfort. Then there are changes to the battery and charging as well.

What are the specs?
The 11 per cent increase in power to 641bhp is accompanied by an extra 22lb ft – taking total torque up to 568lb ft. Top speed is 161mph and 0-62mph is done in just 3.5sec, with a launch control system making this repeatedly accessible. Serious numbers. The all-wheel-drive powertrain is quite significantly rear-biased, with a 303kW (406bhp) motor at the back and a 175kW (235bhp) motor at the front. An adjustable eLSD helps with traction. Up to a point.

Launch control aside, the EV6 is at its quickest with the transmission doing its normal single-speed thing. Engage the new VGS – available in all but the Eco driving mode – by pressing the star button on the steering wheel, and you get six virtual gears with an artificial redline equivalent to around 6500rpm. A digital rev counter indicates as such, though in reality the e-motors are spinning much, much faster.

Accompanied by the ‘active sounds design’, futuristic sort of simulated engine noise in a choice of volumes (including Off), the VGS gives you a driving experience that’s more directly relatable to a combustion car. But also introduces natural pauses between ‘ratios’, reducing the inherent rabidity of the car’s unfettered power delivery. What a mouthful; we’ll get to what this means on the road in a moment.

A new fourth-generation battery pack boasts increased capacity from 77.4kWh to 84.0kWh, and thus the GT now has a longer 279-mile WLTP driving range – 16 miles more. Claimed efficiency is 3.0 miles per kWh but we halved that when we were really enjoying ourselves, and ended on an overall average of 1.9. Performance stayed strong all the way down to five per cent battery remaining, when we handed back the car.

With 800v tech and 258kW peak charging performance (eight percent faster than the old EV6 GT),

title: 2026 Nissan Leaf Review | The EV That Finally Gets Affordability Right #automotive
description:
The 2026 Nissan Leaf is all-new and more refined. Plus it’s more affordable than most EVs on sale today. Starting at $31,500 (yeah, including shipping), it delivers real-world range, upgraded tech, and the everyday comfort Nissan is known for — without drifting into luxury-car pricing.

After living with the Leaf for a full week, I dig into real-world range, charging speeds, interior usability, ride quality, and how it stacks up as a practical electric vehicle. The third-generation Leaf is so improved, Nissan could’ve given it a new name.  Remember, Leaf was a finalist for the NACTOY 2026 North American Utility of the Year.

Here’s what I cover in this video:
• Real-world driving range vs EPA estimates
• Charging at home and DC fast charging (NACS + J1772)
• Interior tech, Google Assistant & phone projection
• Ride quality, performance, and everyday drivability
• Cargo space, back seat comfort, and usability
• How the Leaf compares to affordable EVs like the Chevy Bolt

Looking for an affordable EV with good range, practical size, and modern tech? Nissan Leaf might be exactly what you need.

Join Ginny as she takes a first look at the all-new Nissan LEAF, the long-awaited follow-up to the car that kick-started the electric revolution. When the original, legendary LEAF arrived back in 2010, it proved that EVs could be practical, reliable and genuinely usable every day. But after more than a decade, rivals have raced ahead… so has Nissan done enough to make this new LEAF a leader once again?

In this video, Ginny explores what’s new, from its bold design and upgraded range to the latest tech and driving dynamics. She also asks the big question: has Nissan waited too long to reinvent its most important EV?

Join us as we find out whether this reinvented LEAF can reclaim its crown as the original everyday electric hero, or if it’s been left behind in a crowded field of new contenders.

0:00 – The OG Electric Car is Back!
The Nissan Leaf returns with a new look, new tech — and a big glow-up.

0:39 – Design & First Look
Sleeker, lower, and way more aerodynamic – with Easter eggs hidden all over.

1:09 – Nissan Leaf Exterior & Aero Details
Active grille shutters, flat floor, illuminated badge, and aerodynamic wheels explained.

2:10 – Built in Britain 🇬🇧
Battery, motors, and car all made in Sunderland – meaning it qualifies for the £3,750 EV grant.

2:30 – Battery Options & Range
52 kWh or 75 kWh packs, up to 386 miles WLTP – and real-world numbers that actually make sense.

3:13 – Charging Speed & Efficiency
105–150 kW DC charging, 259 miles added in 30 minutes, and practical charging insights.

3:35 – Interior Design & Materials
Calming cabin, soft materials, synthetic leather, and that “rebranded plastic” feel.

4:50 – Tech & Infotainment
Dual screens running Google built-in: Maps, Assistant, Play Store and smart route planning.

6:08 – Apps, Games & Family Features
In-car entertainment, Tesla-style “fireplace” mode, and physical controls where they matter.

6:47 – Heated Seats, App Control & Everyday Use
Remote pre-heating, app-controlled comfort, and clever usability touches.

7:20 – Rear Seats & Space Test
Headroom check with a 6'2” passenger – family-friendly but not class-leading space.

8:14 – Boot Space & Practicality
437 litres, hidden storage bins, folding seats and clever underfloor compartments.

9:47 – On the Road: First Drive Impressions
Refined, smooth and grown-up — but not quite as sharp as a Kia EV3 or Škoda Elroq.

10:45 – Steering, Regen & Driving Modes
Light steering, e-Pedal mode, flappy paddles for regen and adaptive cruise control.

12:22 – Power & Performance
174–215 hp, 0–62 mph in 7.6 seconds — calm rather than thrilling.

13:26 – Efficiency & Real-World Range
Claimed 4.5 mi/kWh, real-world closer to 3.7 — still competitive for its size.

14:55 – Warranty & Running Costs
3-year car / 8-year battery warranty – but rivals offer longer coverage.

15:12 – Price & Grant Details
From £33k (52 kWh) or £36k (75 kWh) before grants — and yes, both qualify for the £3,750 EV grant.

15:54 – Best Range for Your Money
386 miles for £32k after grant — unbeatable range-per-pound ratio in the UK.

16:20 – Verdict: A Leaf Turned Over
Smarter, cheaper, and made in Britain — but is it exciting enough?

title: So well done but one big issue! // 2026 Nissan LEAF
description:
This is a Motormouth couple car review of the all-new 2026 Nissan LEAF full electric compact crossover.
We are having a lot of fun with the 2026 Nissan LEAF, it’s a great city run around offering good range and torque, it’s modern and sporty, there’s plenty of tech and how about this bold Seabreeze blue colour? 
The 2026 Nissan LEAF has a 75 kWh battery with up to 488 km/303 miles of range. It has 214 horsepower and 261 lb-ft of torque. This is a FWD model. 
The 2026 Nissan LEAF comes standard with dual 12.3” screens, wireless Apple Carplay and Android Auto, a
3D Around view monitor, 4-speaker audio system, cloth upholstery, 6-way manual heated front seats, a heat pump, manual liftgate and Nissan Safety Shield 360.
We are driving the 2026 Nissan LEAF SV+ trim, it adds some extra features like dual 14.3” screens, Google built-in, 6-speaker audio system, wireless charger, regen braking with e-step, leatherette upholstery, 8-way power driver’s seat, heated steering wheel, heated rear seats, rear centre armrest and a Divide-N-Hide cargo system. The top trim includes, driver seat memory, a panoramic sunroof, power tailgate and a head-up display. 
In Canada, the 2026 Nissan LEAF starts at $47,843, SV+ $50,843 and Platinum+ $55,643.
(Pricing includes, freight, PDI and fees) 
In the US the 2026 Nissan LEAF S+ $29,990, SV+ $34,230, Platinum+ $38,990. (MSRP)
The 2026 Nissan LEAF comes with a 7.2 kW onboard charger with speeds up to 150 kW at a DC fast charger, it can charge from 10-80% in 35 minutes. With a Level 2 charger 6.5 hours. It is equipped with the North American Charging Standard port on the passenger side fender and a J1772 port on the driver’s side fender. Standard Vehicle to load capability.

title: 2026 Nissan Leaf FULL Review and Tour /// The BEST New EV of 2026?
description:
Nissan has reinvented the Leaf from the ground up and it’s better in every single aspect. From a truly affordable price, impressive range, efficiency, interior refinement and comfort, and overall style… this Leaf may be the best new EV for the money in 2026. Out Platinum+ tester surprised with it’s smoothness, bug free tech, and exclusive features - like the “ni-san” Z32 inspired 3D taillights, headrest speakers, and electrochroamatic roof.  ⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀ ⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀ ⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀ ⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀

So how was it? 

The bits we didn’t like - no 1-pedal drive.. which honestly is fine, no AWD on offer (yet).

Otherwise, the 2026 Leaf was perfect. The interior is filled with hard buttons, and easy to use. The displays? Feature rich, simple, and great graphics. The leaf even has 2 charge ports with avaliable Plug & Charge, standard NACS and good heat management. (The interior heater is especially strong) It may not be the largest  vehicle, but the new Leaf feels better put together than rivals a size class above. Don’t overlook it. 

ACNCarScore: 9/10

title: The Nissan Leaf Returns with Porsche Taycan Features!
description:
14 years ago, the Nissan Leaf pioneered the EV revolution as the first purpose-built electric production car. Now, the all-new third generation is here, and it's a monumental leap from its EV OG predecessor. In this episode, Robert returns to Denmark—the site of the original test drive—to see if the new Leaf lives up to the hype.

The 2025/2026 Leaf ditches the old hatchback look for a crossover shape built on the dedicated CMF-EV platform. With a new 75 kWh battery, it boasts up to a 386-mile WLTP range (over 300 miles more than the original!) and much-needed liquid-cooled battery thermal management. Robert was particularly taken by the incredibly cool dimming panoramic roof  and the map-aware regenerative braking system. Let us know what you think!

title: New 2026 Nissan Leaf Review - This one is more than likeable!
description:
The all new 2026 Nissan Leaf is here and I´ve been driving it!

When it popped up in 2010 it quickly became popular as one of very few EV´s for the public market. Since then the Leaf has sold over 700 000 units and now the 3rd gen is ready to take on the world. 

Its being built in Sunderland, England and comes with two choices of battery sizes. A 52 kWh with a range of 440 km and a 75 kWh with 622 km in range. 150 kW fast charging for the bigger battery and 105 kW for the smaller.
Yet a little shorter, the new Leaf is still is bigger inside. Sleek and aerodynamic with a Cd value of 0,25 and a consumption as low as 13,8 kWh/100 km. 
Prices start at about 435 000 SEK ( 39 879 Euro ) for the small battery and 510 000 SEK ( 46 755 Euro ) for the larger one.
Is this finally the EV that will make a comeback and show what its all about in its class, or is it just another mainstream product?

title: 2026 Nissan Leaf Platinum+ Interior Review /// Best In Class?
description:
Nissan has reinvented the Leaf from the ground up and it’s better in every single aspect. From a truly affordable price, impressive range, efficiency, interior refinement and comfort, and overall style… this Leaf may be the best new EV for the money in 2026. Out Platinum+ tester surprised with it’s smoothness, bug free tech, and exclusive features - like the “ni-san” Z32 inspired 3D taillights, headrest speakers, and electrochroamatic roof.  ⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀ ⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀ ⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀ ⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀

🏁  @Allcarnews Spec sheet: 
2026 Nissan Leaf Platinum+
75kWh Liquid Cooled Lithium Battery 
214HP & 261 lb-ft of torque FWD motor
150kW DCFC
7.1kW AC LVL2
259 Miles of EPA Rated Range
10-80% in 35min
Base Leaf Price: $29,990
Platinum+ MSRP: $38,990
As Tested: $41,930
🏁 
 ⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀⠀ 
So how was it? 

The bits we didn’t like - no 1-pedal drive.. which honestly is fine, no AWD on offer (yet).

Otherwise, the 2026 Leaf was perfect. The interior is filled with hard buttons, and easy to use. The displays? Feature rich, simple, and great graphics. The leaf even has 2 charge ports with avaliable Plug & Charge, standard NACS and good heat management. (The interior heater is especially strong) It may not be the largest  vehicle, but the new Leaf feels better put together than rivals a size class above. Don’t overlook it. 

ACNCarScore: 9/10

title: 2026 Nissan Leaf Isn’t A Quirky Leaf — It’s Nissan’s Mainstream EV | #ev #electriccar
description:
First off, a huge thanks and shoutout to the fine folks at Community Nissan in Bloomington, Indiana for allowing me to come out and film this amazing 2026 Nissan Leaf!
Their location and contact information is below;
Community Nissan Bloomington
2222 S. Walnut Street - Bloomington, IN 47401
www.communitycars.com | 812.331.2300
GPS Coordinates: 39.13992° N, 86.53178° W

⚠️DISCLAIMER⚠️
***Please understand that I DO NOT work for any dealerships and I cannot, nor will I attempt to answer questions regarding “how much”, “still available” or related. Please use the contact information provided to perform your own research! Thank you for your understanding!***

The Nissan Leaf was one of the first electric cars you could actually live with. Quiet, comfortable, practical — and for a while, completely alone. Then the EV world moved fast, and the Leaf didn’t quite keep pace.
This is the all-new Nissan Leaf, and it feels like a genuine reset. Nissan focused less on chasing trends and more on getting the fundamentals right: thoughtful layout, excellent ergonomics, impressive build quality, and some of the most comfortable seats in the segment thanks to Nissan’s Zero Gravity design.
In this review, we take a close look at how the new Leaf fits into today’s EV landscape, what Nissan got right with the redesign, and whether this is finally the Leaf built for modern expectations. Specs and numbers matter, but how a car feels to live with matters more — and that’s where this Leaf really stands out.

description:

title: The 2026 Nissan LEAF at night is Spectacular - Here's why
description:
#NISSAN #LEAF #EV #TESLA  

The Nissan Leaf has regained its status as the ultimate small EV. At night, the platinum trim shines its brilliance.

The all-new 2026 Nissan LEAF offers a sleek and streamlined design, an effortless charging experience, improved battery range1, and even greater value for money. The 2026 Nissan LEAF will reach Nissan dealerships this fall with Manufacturer's Suggested Retail Prices (MSRP) for the S+ grade starting at $29,9902 – the lowest starting MSRP for any new EV currently on sale in the U.S.3

Nissan's commitment to making EV mobility accessible to all is demonstrated by the new LEAF's MSRP being lower than it was 15 years ago, when the 2011 LEAF carried a list price of $32,780.

Additionally, the 2026 SV+ grade has an MSRP of $34,230, undercutting the 2025 LEAF SV PLUS by $1,960 despite offering dramatically more standard features and an increased driving range.

"The LEAF really kicked off the mainstream EV market when it launched 15 years ago, and Nissan's still committed to making electric driving accessible to all," said Vinay Shahani, senior vice president of U.S. Marketing and Sales at Nissan. "We've stuck to our mission of engineering EVs that are affordable but packed with value. That's how we've kept the LEAF's price steady over three generations — all while giving owners more style, more technology, more features and a vastly improved driving range over the original 2011 model."

The all-new LEAF features a longer driving range of up to 303 miles per charge1, and built-in North American Charging Standard (NACS) compatibility with Plug & Charge4 provides seamless access to more than 20,000 Tesla Superchargers for convenient public charging5, making LEAF ideal for commuting and road trips alike.

LEAF's modern design includes clean, minimalist surfacing and details like motorized flush door handles, a Nissan-first for a production car; a sleek, curving roofline, featuring a katana blade element inspired by the Z sports car; and a segment-first dimming panoramic roof6, which can transition between opaque and transparent at the touch of a button. The airy, spacious cabin features dual displays (12.3 inches, S/S+; 14.3 inches, SV+/PLATINUM+); an open, flat floor beneath the dashboard; and redesigned seats that enhance second-row knee room.

Advanced technology throughout makes living with LEAF effortless and enjoyable. Available Google built-in7, including Google Maps, streamlines navigation with Intelligent Route Planner, and the use of other familiar apps. New-to-LEAF camera tech includes standard, class-exclusive Intelligent Around View® Monitor8, standard Invisible Hood View8 and standard Front Wide View8 functions.

title: 2026 Kia EV3 Review - Better Than Tesla Model 3 and Nissan Leaf? FIRST LOOK
description:
What if an EV under CHF 35,000 delivered premium features that usually cost CHF 15,000 extra? The 2026 Kia EV3 shouldn't exist at this price point, but somehow Kia pulled it off. We're talking 373 miles WLTP range (300+ EPA expected), dual 12.3-inch screens, wireless smartphone integration, adaptive cruise, and recycled materials that feel more expensive than leather. The catch? There really isn't one—unless you count 31-minute charging instead of hyperfast 18-minute fills.
This video breaks down why the EV3 might be the first affordable electric vehicle that doesn't feel like a compromise. From the mesh headrests borrowed from the EV9 to the new i-Pedal 3.0 system that finally fixes one-pedal driving, we're covering the features that matter. Plus, we reveal the pricing structure from CHF 34,950 to CHF 49,950 and what you actually get at each trim level.

Key Specifications:
Starting price: CHF 34,950 (58.3 kWh) / CHF 49,950 (81.4 kWh GT-Line)
Range: 436 km WLTP (58.3 kWh) / 600 km WLTP (81.4 kWh)
Power: 201 HP / 209 lb-ft torque
0-100 km/h: 7.5 seconds
Charging: 10-80% in 31 minutes (400V architecture)
Dual 12.3" screens + 5" climate control display
V2L charging, wireless CarPlay/Android Auto
i-Pedal 3.0 regenerative braking system
GT version coming with AWD and sub-6-second 0-100 km/h

title: I Bought An Old Nissan Leaf...This Surprised Me
