The 2018 Nissan Leaf is still a great starter EV—affordable, reliable, efficient, and easy to live with after a year… but is it still worth buying?

Welcome to the Range Anxiety channel where I talk about my experience switching to an EV, learning about how to charge an EV, and my thoughts on EV news and electric cars in general! 

If you’re thinking about buying a used Nissan Leaf, this video is for you. After owning my 2018 Nissan Leaf for over a year, I’ve come to truly appreciate why it’s one of the best starter EVs on the market. Whether you’re new to electric vehicles or just looking for a practical second car, I explain why the Nissan Leaf is still worth buying in 2025. title: 2026 Nissan Leaf – Looks Better. Finally (ENG) - First Drive and Review - Marek Drives
description:
I'm reviewing the completely new, third generation of the Nissan Leaf! This is the car that revolutionized the electric vehicle market, but in its latest iteration, it breaks away from its past. Forget the hatchback – the new Leaf is a modern crossover built on the CMF-EV platform, sharing its technology with its bigger brother, the Nissan Ariya. I test if the larger battery, a range of up to 300 miles (480-600 km WLTP), and Google Built-in are enough to beat rivals like the Kia EV3, Hyundai Kona Electric, and the Volvo EX30.

In this video, you will find answers to key questions:

Why did the new Nissan Leaf become a crossover and how did it affect interior space?

What are the real differences between the 52 kWh and the larger 75 kWh battery?

How does the Google Built-in (Android Automotive) system perform in daily use?

Can the new Leaf handle fast charging at 150 kW, and is it enough for long trips?

Are the e-Pedal technology and ProPILOT assist systems are better than in the predecessor?

The new Leaf isn't just about a new body; it’s a technological leap featuring Vehicle-to-Load (V2L) capabilities and a fresh interior with massive displays. I'm checking if this "lifted" Leaf has kept the pioneering spirit of the world's first mass-market EV.

title: 2016 Nissan Leaf EV owner says cost of battery replacement may not be worth investment
description:
Neil Turner says he considers himself an environmentalist and an advocate for electric vehicles. Back in 2019, the B.C. resident says he purchased a used 2016 Nissan Leaf electric vehicle.

"The cost was $17,000 CDN including tax,” Turner told Consumer Matters.

Turner says his EV was driving well until this past winter during a cold snap. He says the battery range began to drop a kilometre a second, forcing him to take the car to his local Nissan dealership, where he was told the battery needed replacing.

“We went back and asked, can we have a quote for a replacement battery and that’s when I got the shock,” said Turner.

Turner was quoted over $85,846.55 CDN for the replacement battery. The quote did not include labour costs. “I almost fell down,” Turner said.

Consumer Matters reporter Anne Drewa explains what happened next. 

For more info, please go to https://globalnews.ca/news/11130835/2016-nissan-leaf-ev-owner-cost-battery-replacement-investment

title: 2027 Nissan Leaf Review | Full Specs, Range, Interior and Price USA
description:
2027 Nissan Leaf Review | Full Specs, Range, Interior and Price USA

The 2027 Nissan Leaf is a redesigned and advanced electric vehicle that brings modern styling, improved range, and smart technology to the EV market. In this full review, we explore everything about the new Nissan Leaf including its exterior design, interior features, electric performance, battery range, and price in the USA.
The exterior design of the 2027 Leaf features a sleek and aerodynamic look with a closed front grille, sharp LED headlights, smooth body lines, and a futuristic profile. The updated styling gives it a more premium and modern appearance compared to previous models.
Inside, the cabin offers a high tech and comfortable experience with a dual screen setup, digital instrument cluster, touchscreen infotainment system, Apple CarPlay, Android Auto, and ambient lighting. The interior is spacious and designed for comfort, making it ideal for daily driving and long trips.
The 2027 Leaf is powered by an advanced electric drivetrain offering around 160 to 215 horsepower, delivering smooth and instant acceleration. It provides an estimated driving range of 250 to 320 miles on a single charge, along with fast charging capability for convenience and efficiency.
Safety features include automatic emergency braking, lane keeping assist, blind spot monitoring, rear cross traffic alert, and adaptive cruise control. Nissan ProPILOT Assist may also be available for semi autonomous driving. The expected price in the USA ranges from $30,000 to $40,000, making it one of the most affordable electric vehicles in its segment.

title: 2026 Nissan Leaf Review: Range, Charging & Real-World Performance Tested
description:
The Nissan Leaf was once the car that introduced the world to affordable electric driving — but over time, it lost relevance as newer EVs pushed further ahead.
Now, Nissan is back with a bold reset.
In this Future Wheels review, we take a deep dive into the 2026 Nissan Leaf, a completely redesigned electric vehicle built for real buyers in 2026. With over 300 miles of range, fast DC charging that adds 100 miles in just minutes, and a modern crossover-inspired design, the new Leaf finally feels competitive again.
We explore everything that matters most to EV buyers today — exterior design, interior comfort, real-world performance, charging convenience, technology, and overall value. From its refined cabin and Google-powered infotainment system to its smooth electric acceleration and practical everyday usability, this Leaf aims to be more than just affordable — it wants to be the smart choice.
So the big question is:
Can the 2026 Nissan Leaf stand up to rivals like Tesla, Hyundai, and Chevrolet?
Watch till the end for our full verdict, and let us know in the comments — would you choose the new Leaf as your next electric car?

title: All-New 2026 Nissan Leaf With NACS is Improved in Every Way #automobile
description:
The Nissan Leaf was an EV pioneer but it’s been long in the tooth for years. The all-new third generation is improved in every way with up to 303 miles of range, faster charging speed (now with NACS!) and a liquid cooled battery pack. And it’s affordable, starting at under $30,000. Tom Voelk and Autoblog’s Cole Attisha check out a loaded Platinum + trim with features found only on premium vehicles just a few years ago. It’s not overly fast, but Leaf is now a quiet comfortable cruiser with new found style.

title: 2026 Nissan Leaf | Third Time's the Charm
description:

Listen to the Driveway, a podcast available on all platforms: https://cargur.us/1d5zzf

Nissan was among the first mainstream automakers to deliver a truly viable electric car with the launch of the Leaf in 2011. For much of the following decade, it stood as the world’s best-selling EV, only losing that title after the arrival of the Tesla Model 3. Despite that early lead, Nissan has been slow to expand its electric lineup in the U.S., with the now-discontinued Ariya serving as its only other recent EV offering. The Leaf, however, has continued to evolve, and for 2026 it enters its third generation with a full redesign. That timing matters, especially as average new-vehicle transaction prices approach $50,000, and EVs climb closer to $60,000. With a starting price at nearly half that figure, the 2026 Leaf undercuts much of the market and even costs less than the original 2011 model, positioning it as one of the most affordable new electric vehicles available today.

title: 2026 Nissan LEAF First Drive Review
description:
Fifteen years after it kickstarted the affordable EV movement, the Nissan Leaf is back — smarter, smoother, and more refined than ever. In this first drive review, Matt takes the all-new 2026 Nissan Leaf through Prince Edward County’s scenic roads to find out if the pioneer of mainstream electrification still deserves its reputation as the “Prius of EVs.”

The third-generation Leaf trades its familiar hatchback shape for a sleeker, more 4-door-coupe-like crossover design — but don’t worry, it hasn’t forgotten what made it great. It’s still practical, efficient, and priced for real people, not just early adopters.

Powered by a 214-hp electric motor and a 75-kWh liquid-cooled battery, the new Leaf delivers up to 488 km of range on a single charge, with 150 kW DC fast charging that goes from 10 to 80% in about 35 minutes. The ride is impressively quiet and composed, thanks to a reworked chassis and multi-link rear suspension. Inside, drivers are greeted by available twin 14.3-inch displays, recycled fabric materials, and excellent visibility — a cabin that feels modern yet refreshingly down to earth.

But what really stands out is how *effortless* it all feels. The 2026 Leaf has quietly perfected the art of daily driving. Whether it’s commuting, weekend getaways, or surviving another Canadian winter, this is an EV built for real life.

2026 Nissan Leaf Highlights:
• 214 hp / 261 lb-ft of torque for smooth, quiet acceleration
• Up to 488 km of NRCan range with the 75-kWh battery
• 150 kW DC fast charging (10–80% in ~35 minutes)
• Available Plug & Charge capability via the Nissan Energy Charge Network
• Comfortable, minimalist interior with available twin 14.3-inch displays
• Starts under $45,000 before fees

title: Driving the $30K 2026 Nissan Leaf – Is This EV Enough for Nissan’s Comeback?
description:
Back in 2010, when the Leaf debuted, electric cars were still seen as lame, tree-hugging experiments, and the Leaf didn’t do much to dispel the notion. It was slow, offered only around 100 miles of range and suffered from massive battery degradation because Nissan didn’t fit it with a battery cooling system. A fine enough city car, perhaps, but nothing to really move the needle. In the 15 years since, the EV industry has taken great strides forward.

But Nissan hasn’t. At least, not until now.

After a long wait, the third-generation 2026 Nissan Leaf is here. It now offers over 300 miles of range, a seriously improved cabin experience and a new SUV-adjacent design. Most importantly, it still starts at just $29,990. 

Affordable EVs have never been more important. With the tax credit going away, Americans who could pick up a Chevrolet Equinox EV for $28,000 last year will now have to pay $35,000 for the same car. For some people, that’ll be the difference between affording a new EV and having to settle for gas.

But right as the tax credit disappears, Nissan is launching an EV with over 300 miles of range for just $31,485 with destination fees. For a company that has consistently launched middling products to little fanfare, this may finally be the right car at the right time.

Of course, like everyone else, Nissan is also trying to push its cars more upmarket. So while the S+ model is the value king, Nissan only provided the Platinum+ trim to test.

The motor makes 214 horsepower and 262 pound-feet of torque, plenty for a 4,200-lb vehicle with instant torque. And because the Leaf has a 0.26 coefficient of drag and a tidier curb weight than most, it squeezes up to 303 miles out of a 75-kilowatt-hour battery.

Note, however, that the 19-inch wheels and extra weight of the Platinum come with a big range hit. Top-trim Leafs offer just 259 miles of range. And despite the crossover branding, you can’t get one with all-wheel drive. 

Still, with native Tesla Supercharger support and 150-kilowatt charging speeds you can get from 10-80% in 35 minutes. Not brilliant, but in the meaty part of the bell curve for EVs at this price point, and far ahead of cars like the last-gen Bolt and the current-generation Equinox EV.

title: HIGH MILES / Low cost EV real world cold weather test of 2018 NISSAN LEAF 110KW ELECTRIC CAR REVIEW
description:
NISSAN LEAF 110kw EV 2.Zero (127k miles with FULL NISSAN MAIN DEALER SERVICE HISTORY) ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐

This is a 2nd generation model of Nissan Leaf which was launched in 2018 and is a huge improvement over the original Leaf in both its looks and functionality. These cars make the perfect 1st EV as they are easy to drive and very easy to live with. If cover less than 100 miles a day they can be charges cheaply and efficiently from a home installed fast charger, as seen in my YouTube video.

☀ REAL-WORLD RANGE;

RANGE SHOWING on 100% CHARGE  125 miles (at 7 degrees after overnight charge on home charger).
Our car is the 110kw model (roughly 147bhp) in mid-range 2.Zero spec (Apart from leather it seems to have pretty much every extra though). While our car has the smaller battery for a 2nd gen car, it still had a claimed range of 168 miles by the manufacturer. When new, this was tested by WhatCar magazine and they discovered the Real world range was 128 miles - (it is not uncommon for manufacturers to over state the range of their cars just as they over state the fuel economy of combustion engined vehicles). 

I have personally been using this car as my daily transport and you can see my videos of this on our you tube channel. I have found that with the current mileage of 127k miles it will show up to 125 miles on a full charge which is excellent for this age and mileage. I have found that even in the worst driving conditions imaginable (motorway driving at minus 3 temperatures with the heated steering wheel, seats and demister all on full) the car had a range of over 90 miles (these are real world figures).

The Leaf has a lot of very clever and innovative technology including singe pedal driving mode thanks to the E-Pedal. In this mode the car effectively brakes for you when no pedal is pressed. The brakes on a Leaf also regenerate the battery, so driven sensibly and smoothly you can add miles to the Leafs range. 

✅ SPECIFICATION;

In terms of luxury extras, the Leaf certainly doesn't disappoint.

● Heated seats (both front and rear)
● Heated steering wheel
● Blind-spot assist
● Adaptive cruise control
● Digital climate controlled air conditioning
● 360 camera plus parking sensors
● Powerfold mirrors
● Keyless opening and Starting
● Speed limit assist (displays speed limits on dash)
● Digital and analogue speedo
● Full colour screen navigation
● Touch-screen media system
● Apple Carplay
● 17" Alloy wheels
● Metallic black coachwork
✅ NEW MOT ON PURCHASE

Unlike a lot of EV's the Leaf is easy to drive and looks like a conventional car - Within moments you will feel instantly comfortable behind the wheel of this car. Because it has conventional controls (ie. proper switches for the heating and axillary functions) it is easy to familiarise yourself with the Leafs controls. This makes the Leaf an ideal starter EV - and of course it's incredible value for money!

✅ SERVICE HISTORY;

We have a full set of manuals for this car along with 2 x remote key fobs and the following service history;

West Coast Bureau Chief Conner Golden sampled the new Leaf and found Nissan’s latest bread-and-butter EV to be a substantial step forward for the nameplate, though a few issues like ride, handling and passenger legroom show room for improvement. title: Nissan Leaf S (2023–2025) | Trim Review & Specs
description:
The Nissan Leaf S is equipped with a 40 kWh lithium-ion battery pack paired with a 110 kW electric motor that produces 147 hp and 236 lb-ft of torque. This configuration delivers an EPA-estimated range of 149 miles. For charging, it features a 50 kW CHAdeMO quick charge port and a 6.6kW J1772 port for level 1 and 2.  

Standard amenities include an 8-inch touchscreen display with Apple CarPlay and Android Auto integration, automatic climate control, and a 4-speaker audio system. The S trim also features Nissan's e-Pedal, allowing for one-pedal driving by using regenerative braking to slow the car. Safety is managed by the Nissan Safety Shield® 360 suite, which provides standard Automatic Emergency Braking with Pedestrian Detection, Blind Spot Warning, Rear Cross Traffic Alert, and Lane Departure Warning.

Nissan Leaf (2026) review

The Nissan Leaf is the godfather of the modern, mainstream electric car. Peculiarly, Nissan has languished behind other rivals in recent years despite the head start that it gained with the original Leaf which launched all the way back in 2010 – and it’s hoping to fix that with this newcomer.

The all-new, third-generation model arriving in British showrooms in February 2026 brings impressive aerodynamics, longer range, new tech and a more modern, uncompromising attitude towards family electric transport than its predecessors. Read on for our new Nissan Leaf review.

At a glance 
Pros: Efficiency, vastly improved infotainment, built in the UK, more normalised looks
Cons: Sunroof pinches rear headroom, there’s little for enthusiast drivers, rivals have bigger boots

What’s new? 
The new third-generation Nissan Leaf sits on a modified version of the CMF-EV platform that sits underneath the Nissan Ariya and Renault Megane siblings in the Franco-Japanese alliance. It gets the option of two lithium-ion NMC batteries with usable capacities of 52kWh or 75kWh, but it says a lot for the Leaf’s impressive aerodynamic and powertrain efficiency that it manages WLTP range of 271 and 386 miles respectively.

For some context, the Kia EV3 key rival manages a lesser 375-mile maximum WLTP range, but needs a bigger 78kWh battery to achieve it – and the EV3 is already one of the more efficient cars in this class. 

So that teardrop shape, the active aero flaps shuttering the front grilles on demand, the flat underbody, new permanent magnet electric motors, the liquid-cooled battery and clever thermal management, plus that cool ducktail rear spoiler, are definitely working. In fact, talking of the quasi-Kamm rear end… Is there something of the original 1980s Honda CRX in that black-panel rear end? Or is that really a dash of Pontiac Firebird Trans Am in the new Nissan Leaf? (Now there’s a sentence we never thought we’d write.)   

Styling is always a subjective topic but, to these eyes, Nissan has done a good job of making the new 2026 Leaf look distinct from the many and varied rivals that will compete with this, and while the front is slick but maybe a bit more derivative, the back end is particularly cool. And, crucially, it doesn’t look at all like a Renault. Everything about it looks and feels like a ‘proper’ Nissan, so don’t worry – this is anything but a rebadged Megane. 

The new Nissan Leaf has strayed marginally from its original family hatchback positioning – becoming something more of a crossover with its taller, shorter stance. It’s a tricky car to pigeonhole, but there’s something of a shrunken Tesla Model Y to it from some angles, especially the rear.

It will compete with everything from conventional electric hatchbacks like the VW ID.3 to crossovers like the Citroen e-C4 and Kia EV3, or SUVs like the Skoda Elroq. This is the competition that swamped and overtook the previous-generation Leaf. The new one – being a 4.35-metre long, five-door liftback – must fight its corner much more convincingly. 

That swooping silhouette is all about boosting efficiency and a super-slippery 0.25Cd aero figure is designed to carve through the air with minimal drag, boosting range. For now, this is a front-wheel drive only car with either 174bhp or 215bhp electric motors, depending on which battery you go for. They are good for 0-62mph sprints in 8.3sec or 7.6sec, respectively, when in Sport mode. All Nissan Leafs are capped at 99mph top speed.

The platform can take four-wheel drive, though, and while Nissan engineering bosses said that there are no current plans for now, all-wheel drive could be on the cards in the more distant future. Look out for a Nismo-badged variant later this decade, we’d predict

How far will the Nissan Leaf go on one charge? To their credit, Nissan’s execs were open about the fact that the top model’s 386-mile WLTP claim is unhelpfully optimistic, especially at motorway speeds, so for a more real-world estimate they told us that the new Leaf will do 269 miles of range at a steady 70mph. Which is rather refreshing info, we think you’ll agree?

title: 2026 Nissan Leaf Review: 303-Mile Range & Tesla Supercharger Access
description:
2026 Nissan Leaf review — Nissan’s iconic EV returns with up to 303 miles of range, Tesla Supercharger access, and a bold new crossover-inspired design. The all-new Leaf ditches its aging hatchback look for a taller, modern stance and delivers major upgrades in battery capacity, charging capability, interior tech, and overall refinement.

The base Leaf S features a 52-kWh battery with over 200 miles of range, while the 75-kWh battery in S+, SV+, and Platinum+ trims pushes up to 303 miles. For the first time, the Leaf includes a built-in NACS port, unlocking access to Tesla’s Supercharger network across the United States.

Inside, dual 12.3-inch or available 14.3-inch displays, wireless Apple CarPlay & Android Auto, Google built-in apps, and a 360-degree camera come standard. Higher trims add Bose audio with headrest speakers, heated seats, wireless charging, and more.

title: 2026 Nissan Leaf Review: The EV Nobody Talks About (But Should You Buy It?
description:
The 2026 Nissan Leaf might be the most overlooked electric car of the year — and that could be its biggest advantage. In this real-world review, we break down whether the Leaf is actually worth your money or just quietly falling behind.

The 2026 Nissan Leaf returns with subtle updates, proven EV technology, and a clear mission: be one of the easiest electric cars to own in the real world. But in a market filled with flashy new EVs, longer ranges, and aggressive designs, does the Nissan Leaf still make sense in 2026?

In this in-depth, honest review, we go beyond spec sheets and marketing claims to answer the real buyer question: is the 2026 Nissan Leaf worth the money? We analyze real-world driving feel, interior comfort, daily usability, cargo practicality, charging experience, ownership costs, and long-term value. This isn’t about hype — it’s about how the Leaf fits into everyday life.

We also compare the 2026 Nissan Leaf to key electric rivals like the Chevy Bolt EUV and Hyundai Kona Electric, highlighting where the Leaf shines and where it falls short. From city commuting to home charging, depreciation concerns to maintenance simplicity, this video is designed to help real buyers make a confident EV decision.

title: 2026 Nissan Leaf – review – back on top? | What Car?
description:
The Nissan Leaf has always been a pioneer — one of the first mainstream electric vehicles that made EV ownership accessible to the masses. Now, for 2026, Nissan has completely reimagined the Leaf to meet the demands of a new generation of electric mobility. The result? A bold, efficient, and high-tech EV that feels more premium and capable than ever.

Exterior: Futuristic and Aerodynamic

The 2026 Nissan Leaf departs dramatically from its familiar hatchback shape and embraces a sleek, fastback-inspired crossover design. Its smooth, sculpted body lines are not just for show — they enhance aerodynamics for improved range and performance.

At the front, the closed grille integrates seamlessly with the LED headlights, creating a sharp, modern face. The new light signature stretches across the hood line, emphasizing width and sophistication. From the side, flush door handles and a gently sloping roofline deliver a dynamic silhouette, while the floating roof design adds a futuristic touch.

Available 19-inch aero wheels and a palette of two-tone finishes complete the sporty and elegant exterior.

Interior: Minimalist Luxury Meets Smart Technology

Inside, the 2026 Leaf feels like a major step forward. The cabin combines minimalist design with premium materials such as soft-touch vegan leather, recycled textiles, and ambient LED lighting.

A large 12.3-inch digital instrument cluster and a 13-inch infotainment touchscreen dominate the dashboard, powered by the latest NissanConnect system with Google built-in and wireless Apple CarPlay/Android Auto support.

The center console is now more open and practical, offering additional storage space and a wireless charging pad. Rear passengers benefit from increased legroom, while the flat floor architecture makes the interior feel airy and spacious.

Performance: Stronger, Smarter, and More Efficient

The 2026 Leaf is built on Nissan’s latest EV platform, which also underpins the Ariya. Buyers can choose between two battery options — a 60 kWh pack for standard range and an 87 kWh pack for long-range models.

The long-range version delivers up to 440 miles (≈708 km) on a single charge under the WLTP cycle, making it one of the most efficient EVs in its class. With dual-motor all-wheel drive available, the 2026 Leaf produces up to 320 horsepower and 600 Nm of torque, ensuring quick acceleration and confident handling.

Regenerative braking and Nissan’s signature e-Pedal 2.0 enhance energy recovery and driving ease, while the updated thermal management system ensures consistent performance in all climates.

Charging and Connectivity

The new Leaf supports 350 kW DC fast charging, allowing the battery to go from 10% to 80% in just about 20 minutes. Home charging is easier too, thanks to an upgraded 22 kW AC onboard charger.

Remote access through the NissanConnect app lets users check battery status, schedule charging, and precondition the cabin before driving. Over-the-air software updates ensure the car’s systems stay up to date.

Safety and Driver Assistance

The 2026 Nissan Leaf comes standard with ProPILOT Assist 3.0, offering semi-autonomous highway driving with automated lane changes and adaptive cruise control. A 360° camera system, blind-spot warning, emergency braking with pedestrian detection, and driver attention monitoring all come standard.

Its structural rigidity and advanced driver-assist systems make the new Leaf one of the safest EVs Nissan has ever built.

Pricing and Availability

Expected to launch globally in mid-2026, the new Leaf will start around $39,000 USD for the base model and reach up to $55,000 for the high-performance AWD variant. Nissan aims to position it as a practical yet premium alternative to rivals like the Tesla Model 3, Hyundai Ioniq 6, and Volkswagen ID.7.

Verdict: A Reinvented Legend

The 2026 Nissan Leaf isn’t just an upgrade — it’s a rebirth. With its sophisticated design, long range, cutting-edge tech, and strong performance, it’s ready to compete in a rapidly evolving EV landscape.

The original Leaf made electric driving mainstream. The 2026 model proves that Nissan is ready to make it exciting again.

title: 2026 Nissan Leaf — Japan’s Silent Electric Blade Cutting Through the Future
description:
The 2026 Nissan Leaf does not roar into the future—it glides there, like a neon-lit whisper cutting through the noise of gasoline nostalgia. While others shout with brute force, this Japanese pioneer chooses precision, calm confidence, and an almost poetic restraint. The Leaf has always been a symbol, but in 2026, it becomes a statement.
Visually, the exterior feels sharper, cleaner, and more intentional. The body lines flow like wind-shaped metal, with aerodynamic sculpting that seems carved by electricity itself. Slim LED headlights stare forward with quiet intelligence, while the closed-off grille reminds you this car breathes differently. Subtle blue accents and futuristic wheel designs reinforce its identity as a machine born from Japan’s obsession with efficiency and harmony.
Inside, the Leaf transforms into a digital sanctuary. The dashboard is dominated by a wide, high-resolution display that merges infotainment and driver data into one fluid visual experience. Touch-sensitive controls, minimalist layouts, and eco-conscious materials create an interior that feels both modern and ethical. Nissan’s latest driver-assistance suite works silently in the background, reducing stress and elevating confidence without stealing attention.
Under the skin, the 2026 Nissan Leaf delivers refined electric performance. With improved battery density and smarter energy management, range anxiety fades into irrelevance for daily life. Acceleration from 0–100 km/h in approximately 7.5 seconds isn’t about thrill-seeking—it’s about instant, seamless motion. The torque arrives like a thought becoming action, immediate and smooth.
In the competitive EV landscape, the Leaf stands toe-to-toe with rivals like the Chevrolet Bolt EV, Hyundai Kona Electric, and Volkswagen ID.3. While some competitors chase aggressive styling or raw numbers, the Leaf positions itself as the intelligent all-rounder—balanced, dependable, and accessible.
Pricing remains one of its strongest weapons. The 2026 Nissan Leaf continues to offer exceptional value, undercutting many rivals while delivering proven technology. Long-term ownership is where it truly shines: low maintenance costs, reduced insurance premiums in many markets, and slower depreciation compared to early-generation EVs make it a rational yet emotionally satisfying choice.
On the road, the driving experience is serene and surprisingly engaging. The low center of gravity keeps corners composed, while regenerative braking turns everyday driving into an interactive energy dance. It’s not a sports car—and it doesn’t pretend to be. Its weakness lies in limited high-performance appeal, but its strength is clarity of purpose.
The 2026 Nissan Leaf is not trying to impress everyone. It’s speaking directly to those who understand that the future doesn’t always arrive loudly—it arrives inevitably.
Step into Japan’s electric vision. Drive the silence. Choose the future—now.

title: Nissan Leaf 2026 Review: Bold New SUV Design, 300 HP AWD, 480 KM Range!
description:
Nissan Leaf 2026 Review: From Affordable Icon to EV Powerhouse
https://ev.motorwatt.com/ev-database/database-electric-cars/nissan-leaf-2026
The all-new Nissan Leaf 2026 isn’t just a facelift—it’s a full-blown evolution. Nissan ditches the old-school hatchback vibe in favor of a sleek, powerful electric SUV with attitude. Starting at $35,000 (€32,550 / £27,650), the third-gen Leaf is bigger, bolder, and finally tuned to modern tastes.

🔋 Battery & Charging
The new Leaf is built on Nissan’s CMF-EV platform and packs a 60 kWh liquid-cooled battery good for up to 480 km (298 mi) of range (WLTP). With 200 kW fast charging, you’ll get 155 mi (250 km) in just 14 minutes—and yes, it now supports Tesla’s NACS plug in the U.S., unlocking access to Superchargers.

⚡ Performance That Moves
Choose between a 214 hp FWD version or the AWD dual-motor trim pushing 300 hp and over 400 lb-ft of torque. Top speed hits 160 km/h (99 mph), and the new multi-link suspension sharpens handling like never before. It’s a true SUV now, not a converted hatch.

🧠 Tech & Design Upgrades
This isn’t your grandma’s Leaf. Think dual 12.3-inch displays, wireless CarPlay/Android Auto, ProPilot hands-free driving, and even a 360° camera system. Inside you’ll find vegan leather, recycled materials, and quirky touches like etched Leaf logos on the moonroof glass. It’s green, clean, and confident.

🚘 Bold Exterior, Spacious Interior
A blend of the Chill-Out concept and Ariya, the Leaf 2026 gets sleek LED lighting, a floating roofline, 0.26Cd drag, and 19-inch wheels. Inside, expect improved cargo space over the outgoing hatch, along with a quieter, more refined ride.

🌱 Final Verdict:
The Nissan Leaf has grown up—and it shows. With smart tech, real-world range, and SUV muscle, the 2026 Leaf is finally ready to compete with the likes of Hyundai Kona Electric, Chevy Equinox EV, and even Tesla’s Model Y SR. If you're looking for affordable AWD EV performance with premium features, this new Leaf checks the right boxes.

title: Die LÖSUNG für Mitsubishi Outlander PHEV, Nissan Leaf und Co.? Orientrise CCS-CHAdeMO-Adapter Test
description:
Werbung | Was haben die Plug-in Hybride Mitsubishi Outlander und Eclipse Cross mit dem Elektroauto Nissan Leaf der ersten beiden Modellgenerationen gemeinsam? Alle lassen sich über einen CHAdeMO-Anschluss schnell laden. Bei uns in Europa ist jedoch der CCS-Standard deutlich stärker verbreitet. An vielen Ladepunkten gibt es keinen CHAdeMO-Anschluss. Wer trotzdem in der Ladeinfrastruktur mobil bleiben will, für den kann der Adapter von Orientrise eine Lösung sein! Ich konnte den CCS-CHAdeMO-Adapter mit dem aktuellen Dauertestwagen, einem Mitsubishi Outlander Plug-in Hybrid testen.

title: Nissan Leaf 2026 Review - Bis zu 624 km Reichweite | Preis, Technik & Test
description:
Der neue Nissan Leaf (2026) ist komplett neu entwickelt und könnte für Nissan ein echtes Comeback im Elektroauto-Segment werden. Mehr Reichweite, neue Technik und ein deutlich moderneres Gesamtpaket – aber reicht das, um wieder ganz vorne mitzuspielen?

Besonders spannend: Der neue Leaf soll mit einer 75 kWh Batterie und bis zu 622 km WLTP-Reichweite kommen – ein deutlicher Schritt nach vorne gegenüber dem bisherigen Modell. Außerdem werfen wir einen Blick auf Ladeleistung, CCS-Laden und die technischen Details des neuen Elektro-Antriebs.

title: How to Enable or Disable AC in Nissan Leaf II ( 2017 - now )
description:
Learn how to enable or disable the air conditioning (AC) in your Nissan Leaf II (2017 - Now). Whether you're looking to cool down the cabin or save energy, this guide will show you how to manage the AC settings for optimal comfort.

Step-by-step guide:

Turn on your Nissan Leaf and activate the climate control system.
Look for the AC button on the climate control panel.
To enable the AC, simply press the AC button. This will activate the air conditioning, and the air will start cooling.
To disable the AC, press the AC button again. The light on the button will turn off, and the system will stop cooling the air.
If you're using Eco Mode, the AC may adjust its operation for energy-saving purposes.

title: Este é o novo Nissan LEAF. Pai dos elétricos está IRRECONHECÍVEL
description:
A Nissan acaba de revelar a nova geração do LEAF, que passou pela maior transformação da sua história. E podemos mesmo dizer que é o reimaginar de um ícone, porque o LEAF carrega o título de “primeiro carro elétrico a ser produzido em massa”.

Lançado em 2010, conta com 700 mil unidades vendidas até à data, sendo que foi, durante vários anos, o elétrico mais popular do mundo. Contudo, a popularidade inicial foi desaparecendo e nem mesmo o anúncio da segunda geração, em 2018, conseguiu travar isso.

Agora, para recuperar esse protagonismo, a Nissan apostou numa fórmula diferente e mudou praticamente tudo para o novo LEAF, que aderiu ao formato crossover.

title: The 2026 Nissan Leaf wants to set the STANDARD for affordable EVs
description:
#nissan #leaf #ev 

Nissan has blown me aways with the design of the new leaf. Does it have the range, power, and price to make it an industry leader?

Effortlessly electric: All-new 2026 Nissan LEAF is the EV for
everyone
LEAF builds on nearly two decades of EV experience, bringing accessible
electric mobility to the masses
• Completely reimagined with a fresh crossover design, enhanced aerodynamics
and a spacious yet minimalist cabin
• Simplified charging with North American Charging Standard (NACS) port and
Plug & Charge, plus impressive predicted highway energy efficiency
• Innovative technology with available electrically dimming panoramic
moonroof, dual 14.3-inch screens and Google built-in1
The all-new 2026 Nissan LEAF makes effortless EV ownership a reality. Featuring a
more streamlined charging experience, improved range, a more enjoyable drive and
a remarkably sleek new design, LEAF represents the best of Nissan EV technology.
LEAF’s long range and quick charging make ownership a breeze. It is estimated to
deliver up to 303 miles of range per charge2. Additionally, LEAF can be recharged at
compatible Level 3 chargers from 10% to 80% in just 35 minutes3. Coupled with
predicted enhanced energy efficiency and North American Charging Standard (NACS)
compatibility, the all-new 2026 Nissan LEAF simplifies commuting and road trips
alike.
“The 2026 Nissan LEAF is the stylish, smart EV that is easy to charge, easy to live
with and makes daily driving more fun,” said Christian Meunier, chairman, Nissan
Americas. “Nissan’s unmatched level of knowledge in the mass-market electric
vehicle space resulted in the third-generation LEAF being the most effortless, most
intuitive EV yet.”
LEAF owners have driven over 12 billion EV miles globally4 and 2 billion miles in the
U.S.5, representing a wealth of real-world experience and feedback that helped make
every part of the new model better for everyday life. Nissan engineers used this
trove of industry-exclusive EV knowledge to optimize every part of the all-new LEAF.
The all-new 2026 LEAF will reach Nissan dealerships this fall.

As an upgraded version of the standard Leaf, the e+ model is designed for drivers who want greater performance without compromising efficiency or comfort.

Powered by a larger battery and a more capable electric motor, the Nissan Leaf e+ delivers smoother acceleration and confident highway performance. Its extended range makes it suitable not only for daily urban commuting but also for longer journeys, reducing range anxiety and increasing driving freedom. The instant torque typical of electric vehicles ensures a quiet, responsive, and enjoyable driving experience.

Inside, the Nissan Leaf e+ offers a modern and well-designed cabin focused on comfort and technology. High-quality materials, supportive seating, and a spacious layout make it ideal for families and professionals alike. Advanced infotainment features, smartphone connectivity, and digital driver displays keep the experience intuitive and connected.

Safety is a key strength of the Nissan Leaf e+. Equipped with Nissan’s advanced driver-assistance systems, including intelligent braking, lane assistance, and adaptive cruise control, the car provides a reassuring level of protection for both driver and passengers. The e-Pedal technology further enhances control by allowing smooth acceleration and braking using a single pedal.

With zero tailpipe emissions, low running costs, and proven reliability, the Nissan Leaf e+ stands as a smart choice for those transitioning to electric mobility. It combines sustainability, performance, and everyday practicality in a well-balanced electric hatchback that continues to shape the future of clean transportation.

title: Premier essai Nissan LEAF 2026
description:
Quinze ans après avoir lancé le mouvement des véhicules électriques abordables, la Nissan Leaf est de retour — plus intelligente, plus fluide et plus raffinée que jamais. Dans cet essai routier, Matt met à l’épreuve la toute nouvelle Nissan Leaf 2026 sur les routes pittoresques du comté de Prince Edward afin de déterminer si la pionnière de l’électrification grand public mérite toujours sa réputation de « Prius des véhicules électriques ».

La troisième génération de la Leaf troque sa silhouette familière de hayon pour un design de multisegment plus élancé, rappelant un coupé à quatre portes — mais pas d’inquiétude, elle n’a rien perdu de ce qui faisait sa force. Elle demeure pratique, efficace et offerte à un prix accessible pour de vraies personnes, pas seulement pour les premiers adeptes de la mobilité électrique.

Propulsée par un moteur électrique de 214 chevaux et une batterie refroidie par liquide de 75 kWh, la nouvelle Leaf offre jusqu’à 488 km d’autonomie sur une seule charge, avec une capacité de recharge rapide en courant continu de 150 kW permettant de passer de 10 à 80 % en environ 35 minutes. La conduite est remarquablement silencieuse et équilibrée, grâce à un châssis retravaillé et à une suspension arrière multibras. À bord, les conducteurs sont accueillis par deux écrans de 14,3 pouces disponibles, des matériaux de tissu recyclé et une excellente visibilité — un habitacle à la fois moderne et agréablement terre à terre.

Mais ce qui impressionne vraiment, c’est à quel point tout semble *sans effort*. La Leaf 2026 a discrètement perfectionné l’art de la conduite quotidienne. Qu’il s’agisse des trajets quotidiens, des escapades de fin de semaine ou de la survie à un autre hiver canadien, cette voiture électrique est conçue pour la vraie vie.

Faits saillants de la Nissan Leaf 2026 :
• 214 ch / 261 lb-pi de couple pour une accélération douce et silencieuse
• Jusqu’à 488 km d’autonomie (données RNCan) avec la batterie de 75 kWh
• Recharge rapide en courant continu de 150 kW (de 10 à 80 % en environ 35 minutes)
• Fonction Plug & Charge offerte via le réseau Nissan Energy Charge
• Intérieur confortable et minimaliste avec double écran de 14,3 pouces disponible
• Prix de départ sous la barre des 45 000 $, avant frais

title: Nissan Leaf N Connecta Review 2020
description:
Nissan Leaf N-Connecta 2020 Review: A Comprehensive Look at the Affordable, Efficient Electric Vehicle

The Nissan Leaf N-Connecta 2020 stands as one of the most popular and accessible electric vehicles (EVs) on the market today. With its environmentally friendly performance, stylish design, and tech-savvy features, the 2020 Nissan Leaf N-Connecta offers an exceptional blend of value, practicality, and sustainability. Whether you're making the switch to electric driving or looking for a reliable EV for city commuting and weekend road trips, the Nissan Leaf N-Connecta delivers a reasonable driving experience. This review will take a deep dive into the specs, features, and performance of the Leaf N-Connecta 2020 to help you determine if it's the right choice for your driving needs.

Nissan Leaf N-Connecta 2020: Key Specifications

Battery Capacity: 40 kWh Lithium-ion
Motor Power: 110 kW (147 horsepower)
Range: Up to 168 miles on a full charge (WLTP)
Top Speed: 93 mph (150 km/h)
Acceleration (0-62 mph): 7.9 seconds
Charging Time (AC): Approximately 7.5 hours for a full charge (using a 7 kW charger)
Charging Time (DC): 40-60 minutes to charge from 0% to 80% (using a rapid charger)
Drive Type: Front-wheel drive (FWD)
CO2 Emissions: 0 g/km (emission-free)

Nissan Leaf N-Connecta 2020 Driving Performance
When it comes to driving performance, the Nissan Leaf N-Connecta 2020 provides a smooth and quiet ride, which is typical of electric vehicles. Powered by a 40 kWh lithium-ion battery and a 110 kW (147 horsepower) electric motor, the Leaf N-Connecta offers a decent balance of power and efficiency. The car can accelerate from 0 to 62 mph in just 7.9 seconds, which provides a responsive and engaging driving experience, especially in urban and suburban environments.

One standout feature is its e-Pedal system, which allows for one-pedal driving. By simply pressing and releasing the accelerator pedal, you can accelerate, decelerate, and come to a complete stop without using the brake pedal, providing a unique and efficient driving experience.

Nissan Leaf N-Connecta 2020 Range and Charging
One of the key aspects of any electric vehicle is its driving range, and the Nissan Leaf N-Connecta 2020 doesn't disappoint. The vehicle’s 40 kWh battery allows for a range of up to 168 miles on a full charge (WLTP), making it a practical option for daily commuting and even longer trips, especially if you're able to access rapid charging stations along the way.

For charging, the Leaf N-Connecta 2020 supports both AC and DC charging. Using a standard 7 kW home charger, the car can be fully charged in about 7.5 hours, making overnight charging convenient for most users. For faster charging, the Leaf is equipped with DC rapid charging capability, which can charge the battery from 0% to 80% in just 40-60 minutes, depending on the charging station.

Nissan Leaf N-Connecta 2020 Technology and Safety Features
The Nissan Leaf N-Connecta 2020 comes packed with a range of advanced technology and safety features. The NissanConnect infotainment system is intuitive, featuring an easy-to-use interface with 7-inch touchscreen functionality, Bluetooth connectivity, and smartphone integration through Apple CarPlay and Android Auto. The system allows drivers to access music, navigation, and contacts with ease, ensuring that you're always connected while on the road.

In terms of safety, the Leaf N-Connecta offers a suite of driver assistance features that contribute to a safe and enjoyable driving experience. Standard features include automatic emergency braking, lane departure warning, blind-spot monitoring, and rear cross-traffic alert. The car is also equipped with ProPILOT, Nissan's semi-autonomous driving system, which offers adaptive cruise control, steering assistance, and lane centering for a more relaxed drive, especially on highways.

Nissan Leaf N-Connecta 2020 Driving Experience
On the road, the Nissan Leaf N-Connecta 2020 offers a smooth and quiet ride, as you'd expect from an electric vehicle. The absence of engine noise makes for a calm and serene driving environment, and the car's precise steering and responsive acceleration make it enjoyable to drive. Whether you're navigating tight city streets or cruising on the highway, the Leaf N-Connecta feels composed and confident.

Conclusion
The Nissan Leaf offers a reasonably priced car that looks nice and drives way.
The main consideration with the Leaf is it's battery life. Will you be going on long journeys or holidays, sadly not. 
Will it go you to and from work and just about cover the primary function of a car, then yes.

We scored the Nissan Leaf N-Connecta 6/10

title: SURPRESA COMPLETA no NISSAN LEAF. Autonomia RECORDE com bateria europeia
description:
Para muitos o «pai» da mobilidade elétrica, o Nissan LEAF acaba de passar pela maior transformação da sua história. E aqui para nós que ninguém nos ouve… já precisava!
Lançado em 2010, o LEAF soma 700 mil unidades vendidas e foi, durante vários anos, o elétrico mais popular do mundo. Mas essa popularidade foi desvanecendo e nem mesmo a evolução feita na segunda geração conseguiu inverter essa tendência.

A Nissan quer mudar isso e para tal apostou numa verdadeira revolução que deixou o LEAF mais espaçoso, mais tecnológico, mais potente e com uma autonomia superior a 600 quilómetros (WLTP).

Será que isso é suficiente para o novo Nissan LEAF vingar? Fomos descobri-lo a Copenhaga, na Dinamarca, e perceber o que vale este crossover elétrico.

title: "2025 Nissan Leaf Review | The Most Affordable Electric Car You SHOULD or SHOULDN'T Buy?"
description: Are you searching for the best affordable electric car in 2025? This 2025 Nissan Leaf review dives deep into the latest model of one of the most popular EVs of the decade. We explore the Nissan Leaf's range, battery life, interior features, and charging time, giving you everything you need to know before making a purchase. With electric vehicles taking over the market, the Nissan Leaf 2025 continues to offer an accessible entry point into EV ownership, making it a top contender for daily commuters and eco-conscious drivers.In this full Nissan Leaf 2025 walkthrough, we cover its performance on highways, regenerative braking system, cargo space, infotainment system, and safety features. This car is often compared to competitors like the Chevrolet Bolt EV, Hyundai Kona Electric, and Tesla Model 3. So if you're wondering how the 2025 Leaf stacks up in terms of value, range anxiety, or comfort, this video answers all your questions. We also explore real-world usability, including Level 2 and DC fast charging speeds.The 2025 Leaf might not be the flashiest EV, but it excels in practicality, reliability, and cost efficiency. Whether you're a rideshare driver, urban commuter, or budget-conscious buyer, the Leaf’s ePedal driving experience, ProPILOT Assist system, and spacious seating make it a great fit. We also break down ownership costs, government EV incentives, and Nissan’s battery warranty—crucial info for long-term planning.From the quirky charm of the 2025 Leaf SV Plus trim to the quiet elegance of the base S trim, there’s something here for every EV newbie. We uncover the hidden features inside the Leaf’s center console, its quirky but functional rear hatchback design, and how it’s been quietly improving year-over-year. You’ll also hear our honest verdict: is the Leaf still worth it in a world full of flashy EVs? Stay tuned till the end for our final thoughts and a surprise bonus segment!

title: How to Enable and Manage Cruise Control in Nissan Leaf II ( 2017 - now )
description:
Cruise control can make your drives smoother and more relaxed, especially on long trips. In this guide, we'll show you how to enable, adjust, and manage cruise control settings in your Nissan Leaf II.

Step-by-step guide:

Turn on the ignition and make sure your Nissan Leaf II is in Drive (D) mode.
Activate the cruise control by pressing the cruise control button on the steering wheel or dashboard. This will turn on the system.
Once activated, set your desired speed by pressing the Set/Cancel button. You will see the cruise control icon on your dashboard.
Adjust the speed using the + and - buttons on the steering wheel to increase or decrease the set speed.
To cancel cruise control, press the Cancel button or lightly tap the brake pedal.
To resume cruise control, press the Resume button, and the car will return to the previously set speed.

Possible Questions:
❓ How does Nissan Leaf's cruise control work in stop-and-go traffic?
❓ Can I use cruise control while driving in Eco mode?
❓ Is there a way to temporarily disable cruise control without turning it off entirely?

title: 2026 Nissan Leaf Fahrbericht Test Verbrauch Reichweite Ersteindruck | Electric Drive Check
description:
Der neue Nissan Leaf misst 4,35 Meter in der Länge, ohne Außenspiegel 1,81 Meter in der Breite und 1,55 Meter in der Höhe, der Radstand beträgt 2,69 Meter. In den Radhäusern drehen sich wahlweise 195/60 18", 215/55 R18" oder gar 235/45 R19" Räder. Unter der Karosserie, ich zitiere den Held der Steine, nichts vor denen man sich fürchten müsste, ganz im Gegenteil: MacPherson Federbeine vorne, Multilink Hinterachse. Bei der Karosserie selbst: Stromlinienförmige Gestaltung, bündig eingelassene Türgriffe, fließende Dachlinie.

Ihr habt die Qual der Wahl, es stehen zwei Batterieoptionen zur Verfügung: 52 kWh oder 75 kWh. Abhängig von der gewählten Option variiert auch die Peakleistung vom Elektromotor. 130 kW bei 52 kWh oder 160 kW bei 75 kWh. Das maximale Drehmoment liegt mit 345 bzw. 355 Nm fast auf einem Niveau und gleich bleibt stets die Höchstgeschwindigkeit, da wird der Nissan Leaf zugunsten der Reichweite nämlich bei 160 km/h eingebremst.Gleich dürfte auch die 30-Minuten-Dauerleistung sein, die bei diesem Elektromotor bei 70 kW liegen müsste.

Vervollständigen wir das Quartett-Spiel? Die Top-Version beschleunigt binnen 7,6 Sekunden von 0 auf 100 km/h, die Variante mit weniger Leistung benötigt 8,3 Sekunden für den Standard-Sprint und beim Gewicht bringt der neue Nissan Leaf der dritten Generation je nach Modell und Austattungs-Niveau zwischen 1.789 und 1.937 kg auf die Waage.

Was konnte schon der Nissan Leaf Vorgänger? Strom auch abgeben! Auch wenn Nissan aus guten Gründen im Nissan Leaf keinen ChaDeMo Anschluss mehr verwendet in Europa, besteht die Möglichkeit auch wieder Strom abzugeben mit maximal 3,6 kW über einen Vehicle to Load Adapter. 

Ladeleistungen? Bis zu 11 kW an AC, bis zu 105 kW bei der Variante mit 52 kWh und bis zu 150 kW bei der Variante mit dem großen Akku. Nissan verspricht uns, dass man den Ladezustand von 20-80% SoC jeweils in unter einer halben Stunde erhöhen kann, so soll man unter idealen Bedingungen in einer halben Stunde bei der Variante mit großem Akku bis zu 420 km Reichweite nachladen können, denn der WLTP-Wert wird mit 13,8 kWh / 100 km angegeben, die WLTP-Reichweite mit bis zu 622 km beim großen Akku, und bis zu 440 km bei der Variante mit kleinem Akku.  

Bei der ersten Testfahrt von Olli und Steffen hat der Nissan Leaf übrigens 16,4 kWh / 100 km verbraucht, Testdistanz 120 Kilometer, Durchschnittsgeschwindigkeit 48 km/h. Wir erinnern uns? 13,8 kWh/100 km gemäß WLTP, damit hat Olli auf der ersten Testfahrt um ca. 17,4 % überboten hat. Dabei muss man aber auch erwähnen, dass wir bei Dreharbeiten nicht immer besonderes effizient unterwegs sind. Was ich persönlich mag ist das One-Pedal-Driving und die Einstellbarkeit der Regnerationsstufen über die Schaltwippen. 

Das bringt uns nun zum Preis und obwohl Olli die Variante mit dem großen Akku gefahren ist, kann ich euch heute nur den Basispreis nennen von der Variante mit dem kleineren Akku und hier geht es los ab 37.000 Euro und darüber können wir nun fürstlich in der Kommentarspalte diskutieren.

title: 2026 Nissan Leaf Tested - Time to trade your GAS car for your first EV?!
description:
#nissan #leaf #nissanleaf #ev 

In this video I show you the ins and outs of the new #2026 third gen Nissan Leaf. Is it the affordable EV everyone has been waiting for?

The all-new 2026 Nissan LEAF offers a sleek and streamlined design, an effortless charging experience, improved battery range1, and even greater value for money. The 2026 Nissan LEAF will reach Nissan dealerships this fall with Manufacturer's Suggested Retail Prices (MSRP) for the S+ grade starting at $29,9902 – the lowest starting MSRP for any new EV currently on sale in the U.S.3

Nissan's commitment to making EV mobility accessible to all is demonstrated by the new LEAF's MSRP being lower than it was 15 years ago, when the 2011 LEAF carried a list price of $32,780.

Additionally, the 2026 SV+ grade has an MSRP of $34,230, undercutting the 2025 LEAF SV PLUS by $1,960 despite offering dramatically more standard features and an increased driving range.

"The LEAF really kicked off the mainstream EV market when it launched 15 years ago, and Nissan's still committed to making electric driving accessible to all," said Vinay Shahani, senior vice president of U.S. Marketing and Sales at Nissan. "We've stuck to our mission of engineering EVs that are affordable but packed with value. That's how we've kept the LEAF's price steady over three generations — all while giving owners more style, more technology, more features and a vastly improved driving range over the original 2011 model."

The all-new LEAF features a longer driving range of up to 303 miles per charge1, and built-in North American Charging Standard (NACS) compatibility with Plug & Charge4 provides seamless access to more than 20,000 Tesla Superchargers for convenient public charging5, making LEAF ideal for commuting and road trips alike.

LEAF's modern design includes clean, minimalist surfacing and details like motorized flush door handles, a Nissan-first for a production car; a sleek, curving roofline, featuring a katana blade element inspired by the Z sports car; and a segment-first dimming panoramic roof6, which can transition between opaque and transparent at the touch of a button. The airy, spacious cabin features dual displays (12.3 inches, S/S+; 14.3 inches, SV+/PLATINUM+); an open, flat floor beneath the dashboard; and redesigned seats that enhance second-row knee room.

Advanced technology throughout makes living with LEAF effortless and enjoyable. Available Google built-in7, including Google Maps, streamlines navigation with Intelligent Route Planner, and the use of other familiar apps. New-to-LEAF camera tech includes standard, class-exclusive Intelligent Around View® Monitor8, standard Invisible Hood View8 and standard Front Wide View8 functions.

title: 2025 Nissan Micra EV und neuer Nissan Leaf - 2026 folgt der neue Nissan Juke | AusfahrtTV News
description:
Werbung: Autos sind teuer? Hier findet ihr die akuellsten Leasing-Deals: https://www.rad-ab.com/Leasing-Deals


Mit einem ca. 17 Sekunden langen Teaser läutet Nissan eine neue Ära ein – mit gleich mehreren elektrischen Highlights, die 2025 auf den europäischen Markt rollen sollen. Im Mittelpunkt steht der komplett neue Micra, der als kompakter Stromer mit mutigem Design und einfacher Bedienung an seine erfolgreichen Vorgänger anknüpft. Ihr habt richtig gehört! Der Nissan Micra kommt zurück – als stylischer Elektro-Kleinwagen und die Basis kennen wir auch. Ich persönlich bin heute etwas unscharf, das bitte ich zu entschuldigen.

Der neue Micra wurde im Nissan Design Europe Studio in London entworfen und nutzt die AmpR Small Plattform, die er sich mit dem kommenden R5 von Renault teilt. Zwei Batteriegrößen werden angeboten: 40 kWh und 52 kWh. Damit soll eine Reichweite von über 400 Kilometern möglich sein – je nach Ausführung. 

Gebaut wird der elektrische Micra im sogenannten Ampere ElectriCity-Werk in Frankreich, das eine Schlüsselrolle in der E-Mobilitätsstrategie von Renault-Nissan spielt. Der Marktstart ist noch vor Jahresende geplant und ich kann es ehrlich gesagt kaum erwarten.

Ebenfalls 2025 bringt Nissan eine neue Generation des Leaf – jenes Fahrzeugs, das 2010 als eines der ersten massentauglichen Elektroautos weltweit Furore machte. Die dritte Leaf-Generation basiert auf der CMF-EV-Plattform, die auch beim größeren Ariya zum Einsatz kommt. Optisch soll der neue Leaf mit einer auffälligen, aerodynamischen Formgebung punkten und dann vermutlich auch mit CCS-Ladeanschluss, alles andere würde mich nicht nur überraschen sondern auch schwer enttäuschen.

Die Produktion erfolgt im britischen Sunderland-Werk, das derzeit im Rahmen des Projekts „EV36Zero“ zu einem Vorzeigezentrum für Elektromobilität, erneuerbare Energien und Batteriefertigung umgebaut wird.

Neben den reinen Elektroautos bekommt auch Nissans Hybrid-Technologie ein Update. Die 2022 eingeführte e-POWER-Antriebstechnik, bei der der Verbrennungsmotor ausschließlich als Stromgenerator dient, wird 2025 weiterentwickelt. Verbesserungen bei Effizienz, Emissionen und Fahrkomfort sollen das Fahrerlebnis noch näher an das eines echten Elektroautos heranrücken. Schon jetzt wurden in Europa rund 250.000 Fahrzeuge mit e-POWER-Antrieb verkauft – etwa im Qashqai. Sprich da erwarten wir auch ein Update und ich kann nun schon weiter in die Zukunft schauen, denn der Nissan JUKE - kein Scherz - wird auch elektrisch, denn Nissan arbeitet bereits an der dritten Generation des beliebten JUKE. Der kompakte Crossover soll im Geschäftsjahr 2026 als reines Elektroauto starten. 

Eines muss man Nissan lassen, mit dem Portfolio greift man nicht nach den Sternen, aber durchaus nach wichtigen Marktsegmenten. Ich finde es großartig und sage: Lieber spät als nie, was sagt ihr?

title: Nissan Leaf 62KWH 2021 model | Nissan leaf detailed review | 0-100 km/h | 150HP 320Nm
description:
Hi Im Ahmad shahzad ,PlannerPK is a dynamic YouTube channel catering to automotive enthusiasts, offering a diverse range of content. Specializing in comprehensive car reviews, the channel provides in-depth analyses of various models, covering both new releases and used cars. Viewers can make informed decisions on their next vehicle purchase through detailed insights into performance, features, and overall value.
🚗 Nissan Leaf Review (2025)
⚡ Overview:
The Nissan Leaf is one of the world’s most recognizable electric vehicles (EVs), offering a reliable, eco-friendly, and practical solution for daily commuting. Launched in 2010 and continuously updated, the latest Leaf models (especially the 2023–2025 variants) bring improved range, enhanced tech features, and better battery efficiency — all while remaining one of the most affordable EVs on the market.

✅ Pros:
Affordable Pricing: One of the most competitively priced electric vehicles globally.

Eco-Friendly: Zero emissions, quiet operation, and low carbon footprint.

Regenerative Braking: Helps extend battery life and improve efficiency.

e-Pedal Mode: Allows single-pedal driving — intuitive and efficient in urban traffic.

Comfortable Ride: Smooth suspension and quiet cabin.

Good Tech Features: Apple CarPlay, Android Auto, ProPILOT Assist (semi-autonomous driving).

Fast Charging Support: CHAdeMO fast-charging standard.

❌ Cons:
Limited Range (Base Model): The standard 40 kWh battery offers only ~150 miles (240 km).

Older Design Language: Interior and exterior styling is starting to feel a bit dated.

No Liquid Cooling System for Battery: Can reduce battery efficiency in extreme climates.

CHAdeMO Port: Becoming less popular vs. CCS standard in Western markets.

Not Ideal for Long Trips: Due to range and limited high-speed charging network.

title: 🔥2026 Nissan Leaf Review: 😱इतनी रेंज देखकर आप चौंक जाएंगे🔥
description:
अगर आप एक ऐसी इलेक्ट्रिक कार की तलाश में हैं जो स्टाइल, टेक्नोलॉजी और परफॉर्मेंस का शानदार कॉम्बिनेशन दे, तो 2026 Nissan Leaf आपके लिए एक परफेक्ट ऑप्शन हो सकती है। इस वीडियो में हमने आपको 2026 Nissan Leaf का पूरा डिटेल रिव्यू दिया है, जिसमें इसके नए डिजाइन, दमदार बैटरी, शानदार रेंज, एडवांस फीचर्स और रियल ड्राइविंग एक्सपीरियंस के बारे में विस्तार से बताया गया है।
2026 Nissan Leaf इस बार एक बिल्कुल नए अवतार में आई है, जो पहले से ज्यादा मॉडर्न, प्रीमियम और फ्यूचरिस्टिक लगती है। इसका क्रॉसओवर स्टाइल डिजाइन, स्लिक LED लाइट्स और एरोडायनामिक बॉडी इसे सड़क पर एक अलग पहचान देते हैं। इस कार में दी गई बड़ी बैटरी और लंबी रेंज इसे डेली यूज और लॉन्ग ड्राइव दोनों के लिए एक शानदार विकल्प बनाती है।
इस वीडियो में आपको जानने को मिलेगा कि इस कार की रियल रेंज कितनी है, कितनी तेजी से चार्ज होती है, और क्या यह भारत या पाकिस्तान जैसे देशों के लिए सही EV साबित हो सकती है। साथ ही हमने इसके इंटीरियर, कम्फर्ट, स्पेस और टेक्नोलॉजी फीचर्स को भी विस्तार से समझाया है, जिससे आपको खरीदने से पहले पूरी जानकारी मिल सके।
अगर आप इलेक्ट्रिक कार खरीदने की सोच रहे हैं, या EV टेक्नोलॉजी में दिलचस्पी रखते हैं, तो यह वीडियो आपके लिए बहुत ही मददगार साबित होगा। वीडियो को अंत तक जरूर देखें और कमेंट करके बताएं कि आपको 2026 Nissan Leaf कैसी लगी।

title: Kahon na Puno ng mga modelong sasakyan 0500: Porsche,  Close-up na laruang kotse review Tomica
description:
#0500
Humanda upang makita ang mga marbles na nakikipag-ugnayan sa lahat ng uri ng mga sasakyan - mula sa mga klasikong kotse hanggang sa mga modernong supercar, trak, SUV, at higit pa! Maingat kaming pumili ng magkakaibang lineup ng mga diecast na sasakyan para gawin itong marmol na paglalakbay na ito bilang biswal na nakamamanghang at nakakaaliw hangga't maaari. Ang mga kotseng ito ay mula sa makikinis na kurba ng mga luxury sports car hanggang sa masungit na gilid ng mga off-road truck, na nangangako ng karanasan sa video na puno ng aksyon. Tuklasin natin ang mga tatak at modelong itinampok sa aming lineup:

Ferrari – Ferrari 488 GTB, Ferrari LaFerrari, Ferrari F40, Ferrari Testarossa, Ferrari 812 Superfast, Ferrari California T, Ferrari F8 Tributo, Ferrari Portofino, Ferrari 458 Italia
Lamborghini – Lamborghini Aventador, Lamborghini Huracán, Lamborghini Urus, Lamborghini Gallardo, Lamborghini Sian, Lamborghini Veneno, Lamborghini Countach, Lamborghini Murciélago, Lamborghini Diablo
Porsche – Porsche 911 Turbo S, Porsche Cayenne, Porsche Macan, Porsche 918 Spyder, Porsche 718 Boxster, Porsche Panamera, Porsche Taycan, Porsche 935, Porsche Carrera GT
Chevrolet – Chevrolet Camaro, Chevrolet Corvette C8, Chevrolet Silverado, Chevrolet Impala, Chevrolet Tahoe, Chevrolet Suburban, Chevrolet Bolt EV, Chevrolet Malibu, Chevrolet Trailblazer
Ford – Ford Mustang GT, Ford F-150 Raptor, Ford GT, Ford Bronco, Ford Explorer, Ford Maverick, Ford Fiesta ST, Ford Focus RS, Ford Escape
Toyota – Toyota Supra, Toyota Land Cruiser, Toyota Tacoma, Toyota 4Runner, Toyota RAV4, Toyota Camry, Toyota Corolla, Toyota Tundra, Toyota Prius
Nissan – Nissan GT-R, Nissan 370Z, Nissan Altima, Nissan Rogue, Nissan Titan, Nissan Juke, Nissan Pathfinder, Nissan Leaf, Nissan X-Trail
Honda – Honda Civic Type R, Honda Accord, Honda NSX, Honda Ridgeline, Honda CR-V, Honda Pilot, Honda S2000, Honda Insight, Honda Fit
Dodge – Dodge Challenger, Dodge Charger, Dodge Viper, Dodge Durango, Dodge Ram, Dodge Dart, Dodge Magnum, Dodge Neon, Dodge Dakota
Mercedes-Benz – Mercedes-Benz G-Wagon, Mercedes-Benz AMG GT, Mercedes-Benz E-Class, Mercedes-Benz S-Class, Mercedes-Benz C-Class, Mercedes-Benz CLA, Mercedes-Benz GLA, Mercedes-Benz Sprinter, Mercedes-Benz V-Class
BMW – BMW M3, BMW M4, BMW X5, BMW X6, BMW i8, BMW 7 Series, BMW Z4, BMW M5, BMW 3 Series

Mula sa Ferrari hanggang Ford, mula sa Lamborghini hanggang Land Rover, ang mga maliliit na kotseng ito ay nagbibigay ng hindi kapani-paniwalang setting para sa mga marbles upang mag-navigate. Sa paggulong ng bawat marmol sa iba't ibang sasakyan, masasaksihan mo ang kakaibang paglalakbay nito, na pinahusay ng masalimuot na detalye sa mga interior ng kotse at nakakabighaning mga disenyo ng kotse.

Nissan Qashqai e-Power, New Nissan Qashqai 2025, 2025 Nissan Qashqai Review, Nissan Qashqai Facelift, e-Power Technology, Hybrid SUV, Nissan Qashqai Fuel Economy, Qashqai vs Sportage, Qashqai vs Tucson, New Nissan Qashqai Specs, Family SUV Review, Nissan e-Power 2025, Qashqai 0-62mph, 1.5 e-Power Engine, Nissan Qashqai Efficiency, Best Selling Cars UK, Qashqai vs Dacia Bigster, Nissan Qashqai Handling, Nissan Qashqai Refinement, New Car Review 2025,
Nissan Qashqai e-Power review

The Nissan Qashqai is a true automotive success story. More than four million examples have rolled off the production line so far – and, after Nissan facelifted the car in 2024, demand is on the rise again. The Qashqai clinched the top spot in the UK’s new car sales charts in June 2025, with more than 5000 cars sold that month alone.

To put the Qashqai’s popularity into perspective, the Kia Sportage claimed seventh place with just under 3400 units sold, while the Hyundai Tucson didn’t even make the top 10. So, you’d think that Nissan could now just sit back, relax and reap the benefits of its facelift investment. But you’d be wrong.

Nissan has updated the Qashqai once again with a new e-Power hybrid system which it claims can deliver better fuel economy, greater refinement and lower emissions. It works the same way as before, too, in the sense that the petrol engine isn’t connected to the road. Its only job is to produce electricity to drive the motor.

To find out whether Nissan’s efforts have been worthwhile, I travelled to Spain to sample the new powertrain ahead of its arrival on UK shores this September. Scroll down to find out whether the investment has been transformative.

Should I buy a Nissan Qashqai? Four million drivers can’t be wrong. And this new e-Power system drives exactly the same as the old one. If you can afford it, I don’t see why not. Just remember the Dacia Bigster is a far cheaper and similarly efficient alternative.

At a glance
Pros: Better fuel economy, slightly more power, marginally improved refinement
Cons: Laggy power delivery, handling could do with more polish, Bigster is far cheaper

What’s new?
Everything under the bonnet. You get a new 1.5-litre three-cylinder petrol engine that Nissan states, thanks to its leaner combustion cycle, is more frugal than the old car’s. It has a thermal efficiency of 42 percent, which is mightily impressive when you consider most petrol cars only muster a thermal efficiency of around 30 percent.

The engine is supported by a bigger and cleverer turbocharger, allowing it to produce more torque sooner in its rev range. The result is the engine doesn’t need to spin as quickly to generate electricity, as it now has enough grunt to mash against the generator’s gear reducer almost on tick over. Nissan also says it’s managed to trim 200rpm off the engine’s average speed on the motorway, which is great for both fuel economy and refinement.

To help keep production costs low, Nissan raided its EV parts bin and pinched the third-generation Leaf’s motor and inverter. It’s also packaged the engine, generator, motor and all the technical gubbins required to produce and distribute electricity into a more compact brick. That increased the powertrain’s rigidity by 60 percent and shaved 28kg off the car’s kerb weight. So far, so good.

There’s a couple of cosmetic tweaks, too. For starters, Nissan has attached some new air dams to the front bumper to push air away from the alloys and reduce drag. The infotainment system has also been updated with some new Google software and the brakes have been redesigned to allow Nissan to fit the cheapest Acenta model with some smaller (and hopefully more comfortable) 17-inch alloy wheels.

In summary, this update represents a lot of incremental changes rather than a total revolution. But Nissan is adamant these tiny tweaks all add up to a big difference.

Surprise! They’re incrementally better than before. Torque is the same at 243 lb ft, but power has increased from 188bhp to 202bhp, CO2 emissions have reduced from 116g/km to 102g/km and the car’s 0–62mph time is now 7.6 seconds, down from 7.9 seconds.

Most importantly, though, Nissan’s improved e-Power system is more fuel efficient. According to the real-world ADAC testing protocol, the powertrain now has an official fuel economy figure of 52.3mpg, compared to 44.8mpg for the old Qashqai e-Power. Nissan explained how this also allows the updated Qashqai to cover around 620 miles (1000km) of real-world driving between fuel stops.

日産の電気自動車「リーフ」が、3代目へとフルモデルチェンジして大きく生まれ変わりました。
従来のハッチバックスタイルから、スタイリッシュなクロスオーバーSUVへ進化した新型リーフ。2025年10月の登場から約半年が経過し、2026年1月には55kWhバッテリーを搭載したコストパフォーマンス重視の「B5」グレードも追加されました。

title: Mercedes-Benz CLA Shooting Brake Review: The Sexiest Estate on Sale?
description:
Mercedes-Benz CLA Shooting Brake Review: The Sexiest Estate on Sale?
2024 Mercedes-Benz CLA Shooting Brake – Is It Better Than the Saloon?
Mercedes CLA Shooting Brake Review: Style Meets Practicality!
Don't Buy a C-Class Until You Watch This: CLA Shooting Brake Review
The Most Beautiful Mercedes You’ve Forgotten About! (CLA Shooting Brake)
Is the Mercedes CLA Shooting Brake Actually Practical? (Full Review)
Mercedes CLA Shooting Brake vs CLA Coupe: Which Should You Choose?
CLA Shooting Brake 2026: In-Depth Review, Interior & Boot Space Test
Why the Mercedes-Benz CLA Shooting Brake is the Ultimate Compact Wagon

Mercedes-Benz CLA Shooting Brake Review: The Sexiest Estate on Sale?

What is it?
The Mercedes CLA was Top Gear's Saloon of the Year when it launched. This is the estate version. An estate is always better than the saloon it's based on. That's just the law. So this Shooting Brake has an auspicious start in life.

The CLA is Mercedes’ smallest car, about the same as an Audi A3 saloon. The Shooting Brake is the same length as the CLA, but bigger in the boot and more versatile. It also has usefully more rear headroom, which is good because the saloon is a bit lacking there.

We're testing the electric version here. A petrol mild-hybrid is also available.

Frankly you wouldn't buy the Shooting Brake for its cabin or boot space. That banana-shaped profile encloses less space than something boxier. Estate rivals are scarce though. Think Peugeot e-308 SW, and the bigger and very capable VW ID.7 Tourer. Subaru just recently unleashed its E-Outback; a Toyota version is on its way. Hatchbacks? Nissan Leaf, Cupra Born, Kia EV3. Crossovers? Too many to name.

Looks quite like the old one. Is it really new?
Absolutely. It's not just about smearing far too many LED stars onto the grille of an old car. The new generation CLA – saloon and Shooting Brake, and the related GLB and incoming GLA – are wholly new, and not just new but advanced.

Mercedes has developed an extremely efficient electric powertrain, and squeezed in a big battery for a smallish car. Wrapped in the CLA's aerodynamic body, this means huge range.

The stretchiest of the Shooting Brakes is the 268bhp single-motor CLA 250+ at up to 469 miles on the smaller wheel set. In the near-identical saloon, we've actually done 360 miles real-world in a UK winter, mostly motorway, not hypermiling.

That 469 mile official figure drops to 439 decked out in AMG Line Premium Plus trim. The 350 4Matic signals an extra motor for a total of 349bhp. It loses you just 15 miles WLTP range which isn't much for a second motor. Those two both have an 85kWh battery that can charge at up to 320kW, to add close to 200 miles range (small-wheel, WLTP) in 10 minutes.

There's also a CLA200. This has a 58kWh battery of the cheaper but more robust LFP chemistry. It saves you £4,100 over the 250+, but costs you range. It's a spec-dependent 290-321 miles WLTP. Its charge power is lower but recharging 10-80 per cent time is similar because the battery is smaller. A weaker hose waters a smaller garden in the same time.

The interior is another place you'll find a massive makeover versus the old CLA, with a 'superscreen' that goes clear across the dash. On base versions the passenger-side screen isn't actually a screen, just glossy black plastic with a pattern of stars. Higher up the range it's an actual screen with its own infotainment apps and screening video. It's supposed to freeze when the driver looks at it, because a camera is watching the driver's gaze. We found it didn't, for far too long.

The main interface is AI infused, so it'll ChatGPT you all sorts of conversational info by voice interaction. Like the passenger screen, that's a surprisingly useless feature. The best bit is the online route planner with charge stop timings and accurate range predication, which takes into account upcoming gradients and likely speed. But the same applies to any car with onboard Google-based navigation, from the Renault 5 upwards.

Don't get us wrong, the CLA is the culmination of an extraordinary corporate engineering intelligence in the development of the drivetrain and safety. Mercedes has also paid attention to stuff that matters in the underlying car. The CLA has, for instance, multi-link rear suspension on all versions; several of the old CLAs had a harsh-feeling torsion beam.

You were saying it’s not that roomy?
Well yes, the sleek style does bring compromises, and the rear is a bit cramped across the shoulders and tight for foot space. The boot's not bad: 455 litres below the blind versus 405 for the saloon. And there's a froot/frunk that can take two airline cabin bags. That applies whether or not you have a front motor.

The interior is stylish, and the trim materials are high quality. Switches aren't too abundant, but there are just about enough to give a better impression of quality than an all-screen control system.

title: 11 Game-Changing EVs That Dominated The 2026 NY Auto Show | Cars & Bikes | Forbes
description:
The rumors of an EV market slowdown have been silenced at the 2026 New York International Auto Show. Performance-led disruption is at the forefront, evidenced by the Hyundai Ioniq 6N securing World Performance Car of the Year over legacy gas competitors. This shift toward high-output engineering is further validated by the North American debut of the Porsche Cayenne Electric, a ground-up redesign sharing almost no parts with its predecessor , and the modernized 2026 Nissan Leaf, which now boasts a 300-mile range.

In the luxury and utility sectors, manufacturers are utilizing electric platforms to achieve once-impossible benchmarks in power and versatility. The Lucid Gravity has redefined the three-row SUV segment with a staggering 820 horsepower and 450 miles of range, earning it World Luxury Car of 2026. Simultaneously, Jeep is capturing the off-road market with the all-electric Recon and its more rugged Moab edition, proving that zero-emission powertrains are fully capable of handling extreme terrain. These models represent a strategic move to capture high-margin segments through superior electric capability.

The show also highlighted the maturation of range technology and performance hybrids, addressing consumer demand for both power and flexibility. The Grand Wagoneer eRev (4xe) will offer a massive 500-mile total range, while Subaru introduced its most powerful model ever, the 420-horsepower Getaway, alongside the efficiency-focused Forester Wilderness Hybrid. Polestar further pushed the boundaries of the segment by showcasing the Polestar 4 alongside its specialized Arctic Circle edition, a vehicle engineered with a custom suspension and snow tires specifically for the rigors of racing on ice. 

This technical ambition was validated by a historic clean sweep at the 2026 World Car Awards, where every single category winner was an electric vehicle. With EVs now comprising half the vehicle population in various global markets, this collective momentum confirms the EV is here to stay.

title: Some EV Chargers Work, Others Kill Trust: Carter Li on EV Charging Maturity
description:
In this episode of Field Frequency, Carter Li, CEO of SWTCH, shares his journey into the EV industry and dives into the challenges and evolution of EV charging infrastructure. From reliability and uptime to industry accountability and future growth, this conversation explores what it will take to mature the EV charging experience and build driver trust.

Show Notes:
-Carter Li’s origin story and the founding of SWTCH
-The challenge of EV charging in multifamily housing
-Growth of Switch to 20,000+ charging ports across North America
-Different EV charging business models (CPO, software, ownership structures)
-Customer experience gaps between EV charging and traditional fueling
-The role of real-time data tools like PlugShare and Google Maps
-Why uptime metrics can be misleading and how to define true reliability
-Accountability in the EV ecosystem: CPOs, OEMs, and service providers
-Operational challenges and the importance of maintenance (O&M)
-Hardware evolution and the importance of iteration in charger design
-Industry growing pains and the shift toward maturity
-The need for collaboration, transparency, and shared data
-Market outlook: consolidation, scalability, and sustainable business models
-Why the next wave of EV adoption depends on better first impressions
tags: electric vehicle charging, EV charger reliability, EV charger uptime, DC fast charging, Level 2 charging, multifamily EV charging, apartment EV charging, condo EV charging, public EV charging, Carter Li, Carter Li Switch, Switch charging, Field Frequency podcast, Field Advantage, EV podcast, electric vehicle podcast, EV industry, EV maintenance, EV charger maintenance, EV charging network, EV charging consolidation, EV charging field service, EV charging trends 2026

title: Wheel Bearings 450 - Too Many Brands
description:
This week Nicole is off in New Orleans with Acura so our buddy John Voelcker is sitting in. He's been driving a bunch of stuff including the Ford Maverick hybrid, Porsche Taycan, Cadillac Escalade IQ, Jeep Cherokee and Subaru Trailseeker. Robbie drove the Hyundai Ioniq 5 XRT and Sam had the Lexus GX550 Overtrail.

At Milan Design Week, Hyundai unveiled its new Ioniq 3 compact and Kia showed off the Vision Meta Turismo. Hyundai also showed off the new Ioniq V in Beijing. GM has paused development of a next set of electric full-size trucks and Rivian has launched production of the R2. Stellantis now has pet accessories under the Mopaw brand looks likely to focus on just four main brands going forward.

title: #bus 日野混能測試巴士｜HINO HIMR Hybrid BUS ｜當年的實驗車輛 巴士測試影片｜#巴士 #hybrid #japan #hino @lccf49
description:
Hino HIMR（全稱為 Hybrid-Inverter-controlled Motor and Retarder）是日野汽車（Hino Motors）研發的一種早期油電混合動力系統。它是商用車領域的開創性技術，讓日野於 1991 年推出了全球第一款量產的混合動力巴士。 

技術核心與特點
HIMR 系統主要結合了柴油引擎與感應馬達（Induction Motor），具有以下特點：
動能回收：在煞車或減速時，馬達充當減速器（Retarder）的角色，將動能轉換為電能並儲存於電池中。
動力輔助：在車輛起動或低速爬坡等重負載情況下，馬達會釋放電能輔助引擎驅動，降低燃油消耗並減少廢氣排放。
能源循環：透過電能的充放電循環，達到比傳統純柴油車更佳的能源效率。 

發展歷史與車型
1991 年： 搭載第一代 HIMR 系統的 Blue Ribbon 城市巴士正式亮相，並於 1994 年開始量產銷售。
1995 年： 推出第二代 HIMR，改用中型巴士引擎以進一步最佳化效能。
2001 年： 搭載於 Blue Ribbon City 系列，電池系統更新為鎳氫電池（Ni-MH）以提升耐用度與輸出。

後續影響： 雖然 "HIMR" 這個稱呼現在較少出現在新廣告中，但其技術基礎已演變為目前廣泛應用於 Hino 300 系列（如 300 Hybrid）及其他卡車的混合動力技術。
